EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low centered over Canada and its associated upper level trough extending into the Northwestern US  brought multiple embedded shortwave disturbances into the Northern Rockies. Meanwhile, at the surface, a cold high pressure system from Western Canada moved southward, eventually settling over the plains of North-Central Montana. This combination of upper level disturbances and very cold air at the surface combined to bring snow and dangerously cold wind chills to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Benchland mesonet site reports a wind chill of -43.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front ushered an arctic air mass into Montana on the evening of the 16th/morning of the 17th. As this air mass moved into the area, gusty winds combined with the cold air, bringing widespread wind chills to -30 to -40, with some areas seeing even colder wind chills. In addition to the cold, some snow also fell, especially across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated equipment at Badger Pass estimated an event total of 10 inches of new snow. Equipment at Mount Lockhart estimated an event total of 10 inches of new snow.  Equipment at Waldron estimated an event total of 11 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday May 19, 2021, an upper level Pacific trough dove south through the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin and then became nearly stationary for a couple of days. The southerly flow ahead of this system transported middle and upper level moisture into the state, while an upslope east to northeast flow developed at the lower levels in response to relatively strong surface high and low pressure systems respectively set up over Alberta Canada and Utah.  The upslope flow eventually led to cold air damming and the development of a low level barrier jet along the Divide.  This resulted in strong northerly surface winds along eastern slopes adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front for periods of near blizzard conditions and subsequently sent the unseasonably cold air into the southwestern portion of the state.  Locations from Roger���s Pass northward into the East Glacier and Browning areas observed the worst conditions due to the combination of falling snow and gusty northerly winds. The impacts ranged from severe driving conditions on area roadways to isolated power outages. By Thursday morning, the rain began to change to snow at lower elevations over North-central Montana and this transition eventually reached the southwest valleys later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 12 inches reported at 9 SSW Havre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level closed low moved north through the Northern Rockies into the Canadian Prairies and brought strong westerly winds aloft on the backside of the system. Deep mixing combined with 40 to 50 kt H700 winds were enough to produce a few surface wind gusts at or above 50 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station at 18 E Fort Benton measured a 53 kt  (61 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific trough and attendant cold front brought a round of convective precipitation to much of Central and Southwest MT on Friday May, 7, 2021. Mid- level lapse rates exceeded 7 degrees C/km during the afternoon as effective shear briefly approached 50 kts and precipitable water values peaked at around 7 tenths of an inch. These ingredients were enough for a brief period of thunderstorms with severe wind gusts in the Bozeman area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KBZN measured a 50 kt (58 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 65 mph were measured across Judith Basin County through the day, with the highest wind gust of 65 mph recorded at Benchland at 8:45 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Virginia City station 3.5 SE Virginia City measured a 55 kt (63 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday May 19, 2021, an upper level Pacific trough dove south through the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin and then became nearly stationary for a couple of days. The southerly flow ahead of this system transported middle and upper level moisture into the state, while an upslope east to northeast flow developed at the lower levels in response to relatively strong surface high and low pressure systems respectively set up over Alberta Canada and Utah.  The upslope flow eventually led to cold air damming and the development of a low level barrier jet along the Divide.  This resulted in strong northerly surface winds along eastern slopes adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front for periods of near blizzard conditions and subsequently sent the unseasonably cold air into the southwestern portion of the state.  Locations from Roger���s Pass northward into the East Glacier and Browning areas observed the worst conditions due to the combination of falling snow and gusty northerly winds. The impacts ranged from severe driving conditions on area roadways to isolated power outages. By Thursday morning, the rain began to change to snow at lower elevations over North-central Montana and this transition eventually reached the southwest valleys later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10.5 inch snow total was reported at 6 NE Craig.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet streak moved over North-Central Montana, bringing powerful winds to the area, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts along the Rocky Mountain Front exceeded 100 mph in some locations, particularly at Deep Creek and at the Two Medicine Bridge along US 2. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph spread across the plains, with most of North-Central Montana seeing High Wind Criteria. Of note, several fires occurred during this wind event. More information on these fires are under the episode titled Wind-fueled fires sweep across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station jvwm, which is 5.4 miles north of Waterloo, reports wind gust of 60 mph. This appears to be an isolated gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and attendant cold front brought increased westerly flow aloft and just enough of a surface pressure gradient for strong surface winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and the immediate adjoining plains. H700 winds were not overly strong at around 40 to 45 kts, but were two to three standard deviations above what is normally expected for the middle of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport measured a 53 kt (61 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought clear skies and strong diurnal heating for 8 to 9.5 C/km mid-level lapse rates and D-CAPE values above 1000 j/km. A weak shortwave approached from the west and added modest lift, which allowed thunderstorms to develop of Southwest and Portions of North-central MT. The strongest storms developed over northern Madison and Gallatin counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KBZN measures a 53 kt (61 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving along the Canadian border interacted with moisture and instability over North-central MT for strong to severe thunderstorm development. Surface based CAPE was generally less than 500 j/kg, but effective shear above 30 kts and DCAPE as high as 2000 j/kg was enough to produce scattered areas of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient weather station at 13 W Big Sandy measured a 67 kt (77 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow developed ahead of an approaching Pacific trough and brought strong to severe thunderstorms over a three day period.  Surface based CAPE routinely exceeded 1000 j/kg as steep mid-level lapse rates above 8C/km were common. Dry sub-cloud layers helped produce strong thunderstorm wind gusts and effective shear well above 30 kts was enough for quarter size hail and larger. The peak in severe weather occurred on Wednesday July 21st as the main energy associated with the trough moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medium sized out building was flipped over by thunderstorm wind gusts, some tree damage was reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Pacific Northwest began to compromise the ridge in place and introduced a more moist and unstable southwesterly flow to the Northern Rockies. This allowed daily rounds of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms to develop over Southwest and North-central MT. The period began with discrete supercell thunderstorms developing over the plains, producing severe wind gusts, large hail, and even a weak tornado. The strongest and most widespread activity occurred on July 7th as the Pacific trough and associated wind shear began to exert more of an influence on the region's weather and interacted with the moist and unstable environment already in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported winds snapping off large tree branches, also had pea-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trailside Fire was a lightning caused fire discovered near highway 43 in far northwest Beaverhead county on July 8th. The fire grew to over 31,100 acres by the end of the month and resulted the closures of numerous campgrounds, though highway 43 remained open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dry cabin Fire was a lightning caused incident located in the Scapegoat/Bob Marshall Wilderness area northwest of Lincoln. Steep terrain and heavy fuels allowed this fire to quickly grow in size and become a long term incident and was being managed by a type 3 team by the end of July. The fire size was 3,000 acres and was zero percent contained, but no structures were lost due to it's remote location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Balsinger Fire is located near Neihart, MT and was ignited by lightning. It became a part of the Divide Complex type one incident team on July 23rd.  Pre-evacuation notices were given to the residents of the northern Belt Creek Drainage area and the town of Monarch on Sunday July 25th. The fire grew to 8511 acres by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ellis Fire was discovered in the Helena National Forest about 2.5 miles southwest of the Smith River on July 8th. It became a part of the Divide Complex type one incident team on July 23rd, and the cause is still under investigation. The fire had burned 1,227 acres by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The jet stream began to move over the Northern Rockies, bringing a more consistent westerly flow and lee-side troughing. H700 winds exceeded 50 kts for much of the event, while magnitudes of 60 to 70 kts along the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Loma station measured a 52 kt (60 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow ahead of a Pacific trough combined with a tightening pressure gradient for widespread 40 + mph wind gusts over north-central MT on November 5th, 2021. The 50 kt H700 flow managed to transfer to the surface near Geyser, a location usually favorable for strong downslope winds on the lee side of the Little Belt Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Geyser station measured a 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific shortwaves moving within strong westerly flow aloft brought periods of strong surface winds to the Northern Rockies. The strongest and most consistent winds occurred along the Rocky Mountain Front and the north-central plains, but southwestern areas managed to reach criteria levels as well.  H700 winds of 40 to 50 kts as lee side troughing east of the Divide maintained a strong surface pressure gradient. The most widespread surface wind gusts at or over 50 kts occurred on November 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCTB measured a 52 kt (60 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alder Creek Fire was discovered in the north Pioneer Mountains just north of Wise River on July 8th. This fire grew to 10,300 acres and its containment effort became a top national priority as it was in close proximity to 239 homes and the Wise River Scenic Byway. It became a part of a type one incident team, which included several other fires in southwest Montana. The cause of the fire is still unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moved across the Continental Divide into western Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of April 18th. Temperatures quickly fell behind the front, with strong winds leading to power outages across portions of northwest Montana. A band of heavy snow impacted southern portions of the Bitterroot Valley during the morning of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sanders County Dispatch reported numerous trees and power lines down along Highway 200 between Thompson Falls and Trout Creek. Trees fell across power lines in five areas along Blue Side Road between Thompson Falls and Trout Creek, igniting a few small wildfires. Power outages were also observed in the Heron area from fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With only a few recent light rain events over the last several weeks, worsening drought conditions from eastern Montana expanded westward toward Central Montana, impacting Garfield and Petroleum Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By the third week of April, and persisting through the end of the month, drought conditions (D2-Severe) drastically expanded westward to spread across all but the southwestern corner of Petroleum County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The jet stream began to move over the Northern Rockies, bringing a more consistent westerly flow and lee-side troughing. H700 winds exceeded 50 kts for much of the event, while magnitudes of 60 to 70 kts along the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oilmont station measured a 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

By the third week, D2-Severe drought filled in the southwestern corner. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong West to northwesterly flow aloft persisted through the end of November as a series of shortwaves help transfer these winds to the surface. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shelby Airport measured a 56 kt (64 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through Montana for Sunday April 18th. As it did so, it brought a cold front and associated moisture through the area, which resulted in rain that quickly turned to snow as colder air arrived. While most of the area received light snow (generally about an inch or less), areas prone to upslope snow showers along the Rocky Mountain Front and along the north slopes of the Little Belts and Snowy Mountains. A few areas received over 6 of snow with this system, which combined with the icy roads, resulted in a few accidents, including one that blocked Rodger's Pass for a few hours while crews removed a jackknifed tractor trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln volunteer fire department estimated between 6 to 12 inches of snow at rogers pass. Semi jackknifed on the pass. Significant drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moved across the Continental Divide into western Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of April 18th. A band of heavy snow impacted southern portions of the Bitterroot Valley during the morning of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds behind a fast moving cold front led to several downed trees and numerous power outages in the vicinity of Troy, Bull Lake, and in the Yaak. The Northern Lights COOP reported more than 2400 customers without power from north of Bonners Ferry to the Canadian border and into northwest Montana through Troy, Bull Lake, and the Yaak.

A band of heavy snow impacted southern portions of the Bitterroot Valley during the morning of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds led to power outages on the East Lakeshore of Whitefish Lake, in Bigfork, and at Lake Blaine.  Two trees were snapped in half in the Lake Blaine area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moved across the Continental Divide into western Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of April 18th. Temperatures quickly fell behind the front, with strong winds leading to power outages across portions of northwest Montana. A band of heavy snow impacted southern portions of the Bitterroot Valley during the morning of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter driving conditions were observed on US Highway 2 from Essex to Marias Pass due to the combination of gusty northeast winds and moderate to heavy snowfall. Montana Department of Transportation officials reported 3 inches of snow on roadways at Devil Creek. An estimated 7-9 inches of snow fell at Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought rain and snow to the Northern Rockies. Periods of heavy wet snow caused trees to bend over or break resulting in power outages over west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow caused several power outages in the Potomac and Seeley lake regions. Snow totals included: 11 inches located 3 miles east-northeast of Clinton on Wallace Creek Road at five thousand feet, 6 inches near Potomac and 3.7 inches in Seeley Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought rain and snow to the Northern Rockies. Periods of heavy wet snow caused trees to bend over or break resulting in power outages over west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of wet snow fell across portions of southwest Montana. Snow amounts ranged from 2 inches in Butte, to 3 inches in Anaconda and up to 4 inches, 7 miles south of Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through central and southern Dawson County, sustained winds near and above 40 mph were common through the late morning and mid-day hours on the 22nd.  The peak wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at 11:30 AM MST at the BLM Glendive mesonet site (MTM28).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought snow and gusty winds through the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two distinct periods of wind and snow occurred, early on the 22nd then again the evening of the 22nd into the early morning hours of the 23rd. Fifty mile per hour wind gusts and snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour caused periods of severe driving conditions with chains required from near Essex to Marias Pass on US Highway 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 58 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft that mixed to the surface, combined with a tight west/east surface pressure gradient, resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 63 mph across the Forsyth area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the north strengthened and spread over the region. It's persistence and often clear skies, along with even a little bit of wind led to dangerous wind chill values across most of northeast Montana for more than a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta airport AWOS site (KM75), although conditions were not quite as bad during the daytime, night-time temperatures below -20 and breezy winds created wind chills of at least -40 or worse each night from the morning of the 8th through the morning of the 14th.  The peak wind chill of -50 was recorded at 1:15 AM on the 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero and breezy winds created wind chills of -40 or worse from the morning of the 10th into the morning of the 14th.  The peak wind chill of -49 was recorded at 1:54 AM on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first, widespread winter storm occurred across most of the Billings Forecast Area from February 5th through February 7th. This was also the first and only arctic airmass of the season that impacted the Billings Forecast Area. ||An arctic front blasted through the area on the 5th.  Following the front, a moist Pacific flow aloft moved over this arctic airmass which resulted in a prolonged period of heavy snow. Many areas received 8 to 12 inches of snow with some locations reporting well over a foot of snow. After the snow ended and skies cleared, overnight lows of -20F to -40F were common. Many areas stayed below zero for 7 consecutive days. ||Major impacts occurred with this storm as warm roads initially melted the snow. These wet roads then quickly froze resulting in icy and hazardous driving conditions. As a result, many accidents occurred across the area. In addition to the snow, strong winds resulted in blowing and drifting snow, as well as significantly reduced visibilities. US-191 was closed near Harlowton due to severe driving conditions as visibilities were a half mile in snow with wind gusts to 43 mph.||Two major crashes occurred on I-90 near Park City just a day apart. The first incident occurred on Saturday the 6th and was a 20-vehicle pileup on the eastbound lanes of I-90. Responders were dealing with a semi stuck in the median when another semi traveling east failed to slow down and yield to road signage. That semi nearly struck a Stillwater County deputy before jackknifing and causing the pileup of vehicles behind it. The Montana Highway Patrol reported only minor injuries with this incident.  Another major crash occurred on Sunday the 7th. A pickup truck was hauling a camper that jackknifed. A second pickup truck hauling a camper then crashed into the jackknifed pickup. Shortly after the initial crash, multiple semis crashed into each other as they tried to slow down on an icy bridge deck ahead of the pickup trucks. In one instance, a semi collided with the cab of another semi that was sandwiched in the middle of everything. The driver of the semi that was struck had to be extricated from the vehicle. All five semis were disabled by the crash and two of them caught fire. Just east of this crash, two other semis crashed, including one that went into the median.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported across the Lame Deer area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Horn Canyon received 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the north strengthened and spread over the region. It's persistence and often clear skies, along with even a little bit of wind led to dangerous wind chill values across most of northeast Montana for more than a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Dawson County, although initially in a more isolated way, temperatures below -20 and breezy winds created wind chills of at least -40 or worse beginning as early as the morning of the 8th and lasting as late as the early morning of the 15th. The peak wind chill of -54 was recorded from 5:39 AM to 7:25 AM on the 12th at the Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT site (MTLND).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the north strengthened and spread over the region. It's persistence and often clear skies, along with even a little bit of wind led to dangerous wind chill values across most of northeast Montana for more than a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), temperatures below -20 and breezy winds created wind chills of at least -40 or worse from the night of the 8th into the morning of the 14th. The peak wind chill of -51 was recorded from 4:33 AM to 6:33 AM on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy snow shower occurred late morning on the 27th. This resulted in bridges and overpasses becoming quickly snow covered. As a result, a multi-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 90 just east of Billings. The crash involved approximately 30 vehicles on the bridge crossing the Yellowstone River near mile marker 452. The wreck occurred in the west bound lane, with damaged cars, trucks and SUVs spanning almost half of the bridge. Traffic was backed up for several miles in both directions. I-90 from the South 27th Street entrance to the I-94 split near Johnson Lane was closed for nearly three hours while crews cleared the bridge of wrecked vehicles. Four people were taken to the hospital. Two people were transported off the bridge while two others fell about 100 feet off the bridge. The severity of the wreck led AMR to enact their mass casualty plan, calling in 30 off-duty paramedics. Some people were trapped in their vehicles and a few had to be extricated with the Jaws of Life. Multiple buses transported the stranded travelers off of the bridge with some being taken to the MetraPark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple locations in southwest Phillips County, sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph began as early as the evening of the 21st and lasted through the mid-morning on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds near 40 mph were common for only about 1 1/2 hours, but a wind gust of 59 mph was recorded in the mid-morning on the 22nd at the Malta airport AWOs site (KM75).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Bluff Creek RAWS site (BLUM8), a 59 mph wind gust was recorded at 6:44 AM MST on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple observation sites across central and western Garfield County, sustained winds near and above 40 mph were common through the late morning hours on the 22nd.  The peak wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at 10:48 AM MST at the Jordan airport ASOS site (KJDN).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple observation sites across McCone County, sustained winds near and above 40 mph occurred for only about 1 hour in the late morning of the 22nd, but peak wind gusts of 62 mph persisted from 9:48 AM to 10:48 AM MST at the Fort Peck Dam Handar mesonet site (GGWFP).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), sustained winds in excess of 40 mph were common through the late morning and mid-day hours on the 22nd.  The peak wind gust of 77 mph was recorded at 11:23 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft down to the surface, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Big Sheep Mountain RAWS site (LCFM8), the peak wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at 11:08 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms began across the western half of the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 10th. A few of these thunderstorms continued into the early evening hours. An inverted trough and strong jet stream moved over the area during this time. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the entire forecast area during the late afternoon hours through the late evening hours. All of these factors led to a few severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 2.5 in diameter with the strongest thunderstorm winds being 75 mph. |A different scenario developed behind the front across the Beartooth/Absaroka and Red Lodge Foothills. Very strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface as 50 to 75 mph winds continued for several hours behind the front. These winds resulted in additional damage. Locally higher winds estimated at 80 mph occurred just above and to the south of the city of Red Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded 1 mile west of Ingomar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures more than 20 degrees above normal and a slowly approaching low pressure trough combined with a favorable atmosphere to develop a few severe thunderstorms during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts, lasting for 10 minutes, along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms began across the western half of the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 10th. A few of these thunderstorms continued into the early evening hours. An inverted trough and strong jet stream moved over the area during this time. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the entire forecast area during the late afternoon hours through the late evening hours. All of these factors led to a few severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 2.5 in diameter with the strongest thunderstorm winds being 75 mph. |A different scenario developed behind the front across the Beartooth/Absaroka and Red Lodge Foothills. Very strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface as 50 to 75 mph winds continued for several hours behind the front. These winds resulted in additional damage. Locally higher winds estimated at 80 mph occurred just above and to the south of the city of Red Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was recorded 3 miles southeast of Crow Agency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused a few downed trees and tree branches which resulted in several power outages around Flathead Lake northward to Whitefish. The main power outages were centered on Woods Bay, Echo Lake, and in the Whitefish area with around 1000 customers without power for a time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds at mountain top level mixed down to the surface for several hours. These winds were estimated at 80 mph which resulted in damage just above and south of the city of Red Lodge. Most of the damage occurred around 400 Ranch, south of Red Lodge. Damage included numerous powerlines down along with an estimated couple hundred trees blown down and uprooted. In addition, downed trees caused damage to one vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds occurred from scattered thunderstorms on the evening of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from nearby thunderstorms knocked down 20 to 25 mature trees and blew siding off an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of dime to quarter sized hail in Libby. Some trees lost leaves but no other damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm weather combined with recent rainfall caused the Clark Fork River to flood portions of the Orchard Homes area in Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters impacted the north end of Tower Street and also Kehrwald Drive. The peak gage height occurred at 1245 PM June 5 and was 8.19 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong ridge of high pressure settled over the Pacific Northwest bringing hot daytime temperatures and warm overnight lows. The consecutive days of heat made it difficult to cool locations without air conditioning. Daily record highs were set for several observation sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures ranged from 100 to 110 degrees Fahrenheit in the lower elevations with overnights in the mid 50s to low 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human-caused wildfire started on the afternoon of June 13th. Hot, dry, and windy conditions resulted in the fire to spread rapidly over the next 24 to 48 hours. The fire burned through the remainder of the month and into July. Acreage burned by the end of June was approximately 29,900 acres. The wildfire was located about 7 miles south of Red Lodge. Total personnel on the fire is around 300. By the end of the month, 30 structures had been destroyed. The cost to battle the blaze to date has been around $7 million. By the end of the month, the fire was 65% contained. For more information, please see Storm Data for the month of July, as well as the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong ridge of high pressure settled over the Pacific Northwest bringing hot daytime temperatures and warm overnight lows. The consecutive days of heat made it difficult to cool locations without air conditioning. Daily record highs were set for several observation sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from the mid 50s to low 60s, while daytime highs were from the upper 90s to 106 degrees in the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several live trees were uprooted as thunderstorm outflow winds moved from southwest to northeast across the Mission Valley. Power outages and one report of minor vehicle damage from a falling tree branch were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly outflow winds of 45 to 60 mph downed hundreds of live trees, primarily ponderosa and larch varieties. Most of the trees were uprooted but a few had tops blown out.  The main area of damage extended from Salmon Lake to the Seeley Lake area with isolated areas of damage around the Morrell Falls trailhead and near Condon. Multi-hour power outages were common along with several vehicles damaged by fallen trees. In addition, hikers were stranded by fallen trees for a time at the Morrell Falls trailhead.

Very strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface as 50 to 75 mph winds continued for several hours behind the front. These winds resulted in additional damage. Locally higher winds estimated at 80 mph occurred just above and to the south of the city of Red Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 mph was recorded 13 miles southwest of Greycliff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms began across the western half of the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 10th. A few of these thunderstorms continued into the early evening hours. An inverted trough and strong jet stream moved over the area during this time. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the entire forecast area during the late afternoon hours through the late evening hours. All of these factors led to a few severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 2.5 in diameter with the strongest thunderstorm winds being 75 mph. |A different scenario developed behind the front across the Beartooth/Absaroka and Red Lodge Foothills. Very strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface as 50 to 75 mph winds continued for several hours behind the front. These winds resulted in additional damage. Locally higher winds estimated at 80 mph occurred just above and to the south of the city of Red Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was recorded 9 miles southeast of Melville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep Pacific trough initially brought H700 (mountain top) westerly winds of 50 to 80 kts on Sunday March 28th, 2021, which eclipsed the 99th percentile of climatology over many areas during the height of the storm. A tight surface pressure gradient, mountain wave activity, along with a well mixed atmospheric profile all contributed to transferring the elevated winds to ground level. Minimum relative humidity values fell into the 15 to 25 percent range as high temperatures warmed into the 60s to near 70 degrees. These conditions increased the risk for grassland fires and contributed to the explosive growth of the Greenhouse wildfire. On the backside of the storm, a cold front provided cold air advection and pressure rises for another round of strong winds Sunday night into Monday. Robust H700 cold air advection 60+ kt winds were also instrumental for the strong surface winds and snow shower development for that time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brenner RAWS measures 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent melting snow in the northeast corner of Montana and points eastward provided extra moisture near the surface.  Cold overnight temperatures and a clear overnight sky allowed for fog to develop mainly over the lower lying areas near and east of MT Highway 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Plentywood airport mesonet site (KPWD), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less from early to mid morning on the 5th.  Dense fog was also observed at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM) until 10:00 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accumulating rain and snowfall from the night before provided ample surface moisture just before skies cleared during the evening. By early morning on the 27th, temperatures had dropped into the mid 20s and easily allowed for freezing fog to develop main near and north of US Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Navajo MT-5 DOT site (MTRED), the web cam showed visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for about 3 hours on the morning on the 5th. Times are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported their outdoor propane tank was moved by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

A tight surface pressure gradient, mountain wave activity, along with a well mixed atmospheric profile all contributed to transferring the elevated winds to ground level. Minimum relative humidity values fell into the 15 to 25 percent range as high temperatures warmed into the 60s to near 70 degrees. These conditions increased the risk for grassland fires and contributed to the explosive growth of the Greenhouse wildfire. On the backside of the storm, a cold front provided cold air advection and pressure rises for another round of strong winds Sunday night into Monday. Robust H700 cold air advection 60+ kt winds were also instrumental for the strong surface winds and snow shower development for that time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: N Bar Ranch station measured a 57 kt (66 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific front swept across the Billings Forecast Area. Strong subsidence occurred behind the front. This subsidence, combined with a powerful jet stream,  resulted in strong winds mixing to the surface. Many locations reported winds over 60 mph. Very little damage was reported, but some locations had a few downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 mph at the Miles City Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The regional weather pattern remained relatively dry as most showers were either too light to make any impact or simply moved elsewhere.  Severe and extreme drought conditions expanded from east to west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start the month of March, drought conditions (D2-Severe) retreated from county-wide coverage at the end of February, to be confined only to the western and southern edges of Sheridan County.  For the last two weeks of the month of March, drought conditions (D2-Severe) then expanded again to include all of Sheridan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The regional weather pattern remained relatively dry as most showers were either too light to make any impact or simply moved elsewhere.  Severe and extreme drought conditions expanded from east to west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first week of March, drought conditions (D2-Severe) from February, found only in the northeast corner of McCone County, spread toward the southwest to cover more than half of the county. From the second week of March through the end of the month, drought conditions (D2-Severe) expanded further to cover all but the western and southwestern edges of McCone County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the dry weather pattern continued across the region, the lack of any decent precipitation brought severe drought conditions further west into new portions of northeast Montana through the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start the month of March drought conditions (D2-Severe) began only in the far southeastern corner of Central and Southern Valley County (the Frazer area).  By the second week of March, drought conditions (D2-Severe) drastically expanded westward to spread across all but the southwestern corner of Central and Southern Valley County, which persisted there through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent precipitation combined with colder temperatures and clear skies to allow dense freezing fog to form in portions of Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Plentywood airport mesonet site (KPWD), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for almost 6 hours in the early morning of the 10th.  Similar conditions were observed by the web cam at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accumulating rain and snowfall from the night before provided ample surface moisture just before skies cleared during the evening. By early morning on the 27th, temperatures had dropped into the mid 20s and easily allowed for freezing fog to develop main near and north of US Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Plentywood airport mesonet site (KPWD), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for about 2 hours in the early morning of the 27th. Similar conditions were observed by the web cam at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accumulating rain and snowfall from the night before provided ample surface moisture just before skies cleared during the evening. By early morning on the 27th, temperatures had dropped into the mid 20s and easily allowed for freezing fog to develop main near and north of US Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the US-2 @Stateline DOT site (MTSTA), the web cam showed visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for about 2 1/2 hours on the morning on the 27th. Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough and vigorous attendant cold front brought the first high wind event of 2021 to Central and North-central Montana. The strong winds began to materialize well ahead of the upper level system on Saturday January 2nd as lee side surface troughing over the Canadian prairies interacted with high pressure over Eastern Idaho and Wyoming for a tightening pressure gradient over the wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and north-central plains. By Sunday January 3rd, a 60 to 80 kt H700 jet max passed through the region while a strong Pacific cold front quickly moved northwest to southeast, bringing a line scattered convective showers with it. This is when some of the strongest winds were mixed to the surface and swept through the populated areas of the forecast area. Numerous reports of wind gusts ranging from 50 to 70 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Two Medicine Bridge station measured 66 kt (76 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of a high impact wind event began on Tuesday January 13th, 2020, when a typical brand of strong winds descended over the normally wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining areas. Widespread anomalously strong winds then swept through much of Montana on the following day as a vigorous Pacific weather system and its associated 70 to 100 kt 700 mb jet max moved east through the Northern Rockies into the plains. A Pacific cold frontal passage helped transfer these winds to the surface and cold air advection on the sub-sident side of the trough maintained higher winds speeds for much of the day. Great Falls (76 mph), Helena (80 mph), and Havre (78 mph) all set wind wind speed records for the month of January. The winds were not only abnormally strong but they persisted longer than what is normally experienced, with some locations seeing frequent severe wind gusts for six hours or more. The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT station MTSBN (8 SSW Wolf Creek) measured a 62 kt (71 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thompson Falls airport observed a peak gust to 58 mph at 10:02 am MST. A trained spotter located 2 miles west-northwest of Belknap observed several 90 year old trees uprooted by the wind. Other reports include: widespread power outages, roof damage in Plains, several buildings damaged in Thompson Falls, 2 ponderosa pine trees down in St. Regis, a fallen tree across a vehicle in De Borgia, a tree down on part of Interstate 90 near mile-marker 22, and multiple large trees across Prospect Road in Thompson Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds were observed from the Missoula Valley to the central Bitterroot Valley. The Missoula International Airport recorded a gust of 59 mph, while a station 2 miles east-northeast of Stevensville registered peak winds up to 67 mph. Multiple large ponderosa and fir trees, as well as powerlines, were reported down across the area.  Close to 10,000 customers were without power at one point during the storm. One electric company reported 38 power poles broken in Missoula County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During this event a Montana Department of Transportation wind sensor, located north of Avon, observed an incredibly extended period of high winds from 9 am to 3 pm MST. Peak wind speeds observed during the event included: 80 mph Avon North, 78 mph at MacDonald Pass before the sensor stopped reporting, 67 mph Deer Lodge, and 59 mph Bearmouth.

The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dillon Airport measured a 58 kt (67 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clearwater RAWS reported a peak wind of 63 mph at 11:30 am MST. A private weather station in east Bonner reported a peak wind of 54 mph, and a Montana Department of Transportation sensor at Bearmouth reported a peak wind of 59 mph. Several thousand customers were without power from Condon in the north, through| Seeley Lake, to Bonner and Clinton in the south. Multiple downed trees were reported between Condon and Clearwater Junction. One electric company had to use a helicopter, in tandem with employees hiking in, to replace a power pole near Cramer Creek off of Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough and vigorous attendant cold front brought the first high wind event of 2021 to Central and North-central Montana. The strong winds began to materialize well ahead of the upper level system on Saturday January 2nd as lee side surface troughing over the Canadian prairies interacted with high pressure over Eastern Idaho and Wyoming for a tightening pressure gradient over the wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and north-central plains. By Sunday January 3rd, a 60 to 80 kt H700 jet max passed through the region while a strong Pacific cold front quickly moved northwest to southeast, bringing a line scattered convective showers with it. This is when some of the strongest winds were mixed to the surface and swept through the populated areas of the forecast area. Numerous reports of wind gusts ranging from 50 to 70 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Geyser station (2 SSE Geyser) measured a 56 kt (65 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the area surrounding and including the Little Rocky Mountains of southwestern Phillips County, sudden severe wind gusts began at 11:21 AM MST on the 13th, and persisted at least each hour through all the rest of that day and through 1:00 AM MST on the 14th. At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), the peak wind gust of 73 mph was recorded both around 2:00 PM MST and 8:30 PM MST on the 13th.  After winds subsided somewhat through the morning of the 14th, sustained winds of at least 40 mph returned for more than 3 hours during the afternoon of the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With precipitation totals well below normal through this month, the winter drought over the eastern edge of Montana expanded further toward the west, now entering into portions of McCone, Dawson, and Prairie Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought Conditions (D2-Severe) over roughly the eastern third of Dawson County began on January 26th and persisted there through the remainder of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across multiple locations in central and southeast Phillips County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and wind gusts of at least 58 mph were recorded for about 2 1/2 hours in the early afternoon of the 13th.  The peak wind gust of 71 mph was measured at the Saco US-2 DOT site (MTSCO) around 2:30 PM MST on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), sustained winds near or greater than 40 mph were common for about 1 1/2 hours the the late morning, then for about 30 minutes during the mid afternoon.  The peak wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at 3:09 PM MST and the final severe wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 3:15 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 13, 2021, a particularly strong wind event impacted the entire Billings Forecast Area with strong, damaging winds, not only along the foothills (where strong winds are common), but also across the lower elevations of south central and southeast Montana. These strong winds were associated with a cold frontal passage and lasted for several hours. ||The arrival of a Pacific cold front on the 13th was followed by a period of subsidence and very strong winds across the lower elevations. Aloft, a strong northwesterly oriented jet stream was driving through the northern Rockies, resulting in strong winds at mountain top level. In addition, the cold front also produced light shower activity which enhanced the gustiness of the surface winds. The combination of all these ingredients resulted in the very strong winds. Musselshell County Department of Emergency Services reported the loss of three primary residences and several secondary structures.||The following wind gust records at local airports occurred:|The peak wind gusts at Billings (69 mph) and Miles City (75 mph) were each records for January. Sheridan (67 mph) reported its 4th highest January gust. The following day (January 14th) Miles City reported a peak gust of 66 mph, which ranks 2nd for January.||The following is a list of impacts that occurred during this high wind event:|-Many large trees blown down|-Many power lines downed resulting in power outages. |-Several semis blown over|-Frequent reports of near zero visibility in blowing dust, resulting in severe driving conditions|-Scattered minor property damage|-Several grass fires|-A United Airlines flight was diverted from Billings to Bozeman|-Montana Department of Transportation reported 35-40 incidents statewide and a high volume of calls (1000).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Bluff Creek RAWS site (BLUM8), strong sustained winds and severe wind gusts were common for most of the afternoon and evening on the 13th.  The peak wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at 4:44 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At multiple locations across central and southern Valley County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph began as early as 1:35 PM MST on the 13th, and ended as late as 3:20 PM MST on the 14th, with some hours of weaker winds interspersed overnight.  The peak wind gust of 79 mph was recorded at the Glasgow airport ASOS site (KGGW) at 9:05 PM MST on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At multiple sites across McCone County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and wind gusts of at least 58 mph lasted at least intermittently from the afternoon of the 13th to the afternoon of the 14th. The peak wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at the Fort Peck Dam handar mesonet site at 10:50 PM MST on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph began as early at 11:41 AM MST and lasted through 5:52 PM MST on the 13th across Petroleum County.  The peak wind gust of 72 mph was recorded at 2:41 PM MST on the 13th at the Dry Blood Creek RAWS site (DBCM8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From multiple locations across Garfield county, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph began as early as 12:08 MST on the 13th.  After intermittently pausing and returning over the following day or so, it ended as late as 3:43 PM MST on the 14th.  The peak wind gust of 88 mph was recorded at 2:08 PM MST on the 13th at the South Sawmill Creek RAWS site (SSMM8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of a high impact wind event began on Tuesday January 13th, 2020, when a typical brand of strong winds descended over the normally wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining areas. Widespread anomalously strong winds then swept through much of Montana on the following day as a vigorous Pacific weather system and its associated 70 to 100 kt 700 mb jet max moved east through the Northern Rockies into the plains. A Pacific cold frontal passage helped transfer these winds to the surface and cold air advection on the sub-sident side of the trough maintained higher winds speeds for much of the day. Great Falls (76 mph), Helena (80 mph), and Havre (78 mph) all set wind wind speed records for the month of January. The winds were not only abnormally strong but they persisted longer than what is normally experienced, with some locations seeing frequent severe wind gusts for six hours or more. The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Great Falls Airport measured a 66 kt (76 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving shortwave brought 50 to 70 kt H700 winds through the region. The nocturnal timing of peak winds aloft lead to the reliance on mountain wave activity to transfer these winds to the surface. Still high wind gusts were observed over the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of Central/North-central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer at 10 S Chinook measured a 63 kt (72 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

Aloft, a strong northwesterly oriented jet stream was driving through the northern Rockies, resulting in strong winds at mountain top level. In addition, the cold front also produced light shower activity which enhanced the gustiness of the surface winds. The combination of all these ingredients resulted in the very strong winds. Westerly, wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were common, and gusts exceeded 90 mph at some mountain sites. ||In addition to the wind, intense drying occurred behind the front and resulted in the development and rapid spread of a few grass fires. The largest fire was in Musselshell County near North Delphia Road just east of Roundup. Musselshell County Department of Emergency Services reported the loss of three primary residences and several secondary structures.||The following wind gust records at local airports occurred:|The peak wind gusts at Billings (69 mph) and Miles City (75 mph) were each records for January. Sheridan (67 mph) reported its 4th highest January gust. The following day (January 14th) Miles City reported a peak gust of 66 mph, which ranks 2nd for January.||The following is a list of impacts that occurred during this high wind event:|-Many large trees blown down|-Many power lines downed resulting in power outages. |-Several semis blown over|-Frequent reports of near zero visibility in blowing dust, resulting in severe driving conditions|-Scattered minor property damage|-Several grass fires|-A United Airlines flight was diverted from Billings to Bozeman|-Montana Department of Transportation reported 35-40 incidents statewide and a high volume of calls (1000).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted 58 to 73 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary low pressure surface trough, warm moist air from the southeast and a strong cold front from the west all combined to allowed many severe thunderstorms to develop and strengthen through the afternoon and evening hours. Later that evening and night, larger-scale post-frontal non-thunderstorm high winds brought additional damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richland County DES Coordinator conducted a storm survey the morning after the storm and provided multiple reports and photos of straight-line wind damage in the town of Fairview with all debris lying in the same direction.  Large spruce and cottonwood trees were uprooted; a metal building was caved in; and large tree branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across central and eastern Daniels County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph began as early as 1:43 PM MST on the 13th and lasted as long a 2:41 PM MST on the 14th.  The peak wind gust of 75 mph was recorded from 11:26 PM to 11:31 PM MST on the night of the 13th at the Navajo MT-5 DOT site (MTRED).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph and wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were common across multiple Sheridan County locations from the afternoon of the 13th into the afternoon of the 14th.  The peak wind gust of 85 mph was recorded at 11:11 PM MST on the 13th at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple locations across eastern Roosevelt County sustained winds of at least 40 mph and wind gusts of at least 58 mph occurred mainly from the evening through the late night of the 13th, but lingered intermittently through the morning of the 14th as well.  The peak wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at the US-2 @Stateline DOT site (MTSTA).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph occurred at least steadily each hour from the afternoon of the 13th, to a bit less regularly through the afternoon of the 14th.  The peak wind gust of 74 mph was measured from 11:16 PM to 11:19 PM MST on the 13th at the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site (MTMCD).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of a high impact wind event began on Tuesday January 13th, 2020, when a typical brand of strong winds descended over the normally wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining areas. Widespread anomalously strong winds then swept through much of Montana on the following day as a vigorous Pacific weather system and its associated 70 to 100 kt 700 mb jet max moved east through the Northern Rockies into the plains. A Pacific cold frontal passage helped transfer these winds to the surface and cold air advection on the sub-sident side of the trough maintained higher winds speeds for much of the day. Great Falls (76 mph), Helena (80 mph), and Havre (78 mph) all set wind wind speed records for the month of January. The winds were not only abnormally strong but they persisted longer than what is normally experienced, with some locations seeing frequent severe wind gusts for six hours or more. The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Loma station (1 N Loma) measured a 57 kt (66 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving shortwave brought 50 to 70 kt H700 winds through the region. The nocturnal timing of peak winds aloft lead to the reliance on mountain wave activity to transfer these winds to the surface. Still high wind gusts were observed over the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of Central/North-central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station at 4 SSW Ulm measured a 53 kt (61 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple locations across Richland County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph were common at least from the afternoon of the 13th into the overnight hours early on the 14th.  The peak wind gusts of 61 mph were recorded both at 7:01 PM MST on the 13th at the Badger Creek mesonet site (F2498), and from 11:53 PM MST on the 13th to 12:13 AM MST on the 14th at the Sioux Pass MT-16 DOT site (MTSOO).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent, multiple-hour periods of sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph were recorded from the afternoon of the 13th to the afternoon of the 14th.  The peak wind gusts of 70 mph were measured at the Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT site (MTLND) from 11:18 PM to 11:23 PM MST on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of the 13th to the afternoon of the 14th, intermittent, multiple-hour periods of sustained winds of at least 40 mph and/or wind gusts of at least 58 mph were measured across Prairie County.  The peak wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at 12:08 AM MST on the 14th at the Big Sheep Mountain RAWS site (LCFM8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low-level jet stream pushed a short-wave disturbance through our region, which easily brought those stronger winds all the way down to the surface and spread across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of the 13th through the entire day and night of the 14th, a nearly continuous period of sustained winds in excess of 40 mph and wind gusts well in excess of 58 mph were recorded in western Wibaux County.  The peak wind gusts of 81 mph were measured from 12:17 AM to 12:23 AM MST on the 14th at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low-level inversion weakened enough during the night-time hours to allow the stronger winds from aloft reach all the way down to the surface for the higher elevations of southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of at least 58 mph began during the night of the 19th, and intermittently lasted through the overnight hours into the 20th.  The peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at the Zortman RAWS site (ALDM8) at 12:32 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of a high impact wind event began on Tuesday January 13th, 2020, when a typical brand of strong winds descended over the normally wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining areas. Widespread anomalously strong winds then swept through much of Montana on the following day as a vigorous Pacific weather system and its associated 70 to 100 kt 700 mb jet max moved east through the Northern Rockies into the plains. A Pacific cold frontal passage helped transfer these winds to the surface and cold air advection on the sub-sident side of the trough maintained higher winds speeds for much of the day. Great Falls (76 mph), Helena (80 mph), and Havre (78 mph) all set wind wind speed records for the month of January. The winds were not only abnormally strong but they persisted longer than what is normally experienced, with some locations seeing frequent severe wind gusts for six hours or more. The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at 2 WSW Moore measured a 82 kt (94 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 13, 2021, a particularly strong wind event impacted the entire Billings Forecast Area with strong, damaging winds, not only along the foothills (where strong winds are common), but also across the lower elevations of south central and southeast Montana. These strong winds were associated with a cold frontal passage and lasted for several hours. ||The arrival of a Pacific cold front on the 13th was followed by a period of subsidence and very strong winds across the lower elevations. Aloft, a strong northwesterly oriented jet stream was driving through the northern Rockies, resulting in strong winds at mountain top level. In addition, the cold front also produced light shower activity which enhanced the gustiness of the surface winds. The combination of all these ingredients resulted in the very strong winds. Westerly, wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were common, and gusts exceeded 90 mph at some mountain sites. ||In addition to the wind, intense drying occurred behind the front and resulted in the development and rapid spread of a few grass fires. The largest fire was in Musselshell County near North Delphia Road just east of Roundup. Musselshell County Department of Emergency Services reported the loss of three primary residences and several secondary structures.||The following wind gust records at local airports occurred:|The peak wind gusts at Billings (69 mph) and Miles City (75 mph) were each records for January. Sheridan (67 mph) reported its 4th highest January gust. The following day (January 14th) Miles City reported a peak gust of 66 mph, which ranks 2nd for January.||The following is a list of impacts that occurred during this high wind event:|-Many large trees blown down|-Many power lines downed resulting in power outages. |-Several semis blown over|-Frequent reports of near zero visibility in blowing dust, resulting in severe driving conditions|-Scattered minor property damage|-Several grass fires|-A United Airlines flight was diverted from Billings to Bozeman|-Montana Department of Transportation reported 35-40 incidents statewide and a high volume of calls (1000).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60+ mph occurred across the area. An outbuilding in Joliet was destroyed. In addition, irrigation pivots were tipped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent long-term trace precipitation through the last few weeks of January combined with a suddenly cold air mass and clearing skies to allow dense freezing fog to develop and persist for a few lower elevation locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta airport AWOS site (KM75), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less from the evening of the 30th into the midnight hour on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved southeast from British Columbia/Alberta into Montana on the afternoon of Sunday, January 17th, 2021. This ushered in a 24 period of moist and unsettled flow aloft. Most lower elevations remained dry but moderate to heavy snow fell over the Little Belt Mountains and some of the north/northwesterly upslope foothill areas of Judith Basin and Fergus Counties. Gusty surface winds reduced visibility and made travel difficult, especially for highway 89 from Monarch to King's Hill. Snow totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches over the foothill areas to 12 to 24 inches over the higher mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at 7 SSE Neihart reported a snowfall total of 24 inches, snowfall rates briefly reached up to 4 inches per hour between 4 and 5 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough and vigorous attendant cold front brought the first high wind event of 2021 to Central and North-central Montana. The strong winds began to materialize well ahead of the upper level system on Saturday January 2nd as lee side surface troughing over the Canadian prairies interacted with high pressure over Eastern Idaho and Wyoming for a tightening pressure gradient over the wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and north-central plains. By Sunday January 3rd, a 60 to 80 kt H700 jet max passed through the region while a strong Pacific cold front quickly moved northwest to southeast, bringing a line scattered convective showers with it. This is when some of the strongest winds were mixed to the surface and swept through the populated areas of the forecast area. Numerous reports of wind gusts ranging from 50 to 70 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Great Falls Airport measured a 54 kt (62 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary low pressure surface trough, warm moist air from the southeast and a strong cold front from the west all combined to allowed many severe thunderstorms to develop and strengthen through the afternoon and evening hours. Later that evening and night, larger-scale post-frontal non-thunderstorm high winds brought additional damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, an estimated 90 mph thunderstorm wind gust.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasonal weather pattern continued to steer most precipitation away from the northeast corner and the eastern edge of Montana through the month. For some locations, severe drought conditions crept slightly further toward the west by the last week on the month (Wibaux, Richland, and Western Roosevelt Counties).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2-Severe) persisted across all of Sheridan County through the entire month of January.

For some locations, severe drought conditions crept slightly further toward the west by the last week on the month (Wibaux, Richland, and Western Roosevelt Counties).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2-Severe) persisted over central and northeastern portions of western Roosevelt County through the entire month.  During the last week of the month, drought conditions (D2-Severe) expanded westward into all but the western edge of western Roosevelt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough and vigorous attendant cold front brought the first high wind event of 2021 to Central and North-central Montana. The strong winds began to materialize well ahead of the upper level system on Saturday January 2nd as lee side surface troughing over the Canadian prairies interacted with high pressure over Eastern Idaho and Wyoming for a tightening pressure gradient over the wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and north-central plains. By Sunday January 3rd, a 60 to 80 kt H700 jet max passed through the region while a strong Pacific cold front quickly moved northwest to southeast, bringing a line scattered convective showers with it. This is when some of the strongest winds were mixed to the surface and swept through the populated areas of the forecast area. Numerous reports of wind gusts ranging from 50 to 70 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Loma station (8 ESE Fort Benton) measured 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of a high impact wind event began on Tuesday January 13th, 2020, when a typical brand of strong winds descended over the normally wind prone Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining areas. Widespread anomalously strong winds then swept through much of Montana on the following day as a vigorous Pacific weather system and its associated 70 to 100 kt 700 mb jet max moved east through the Northern Rockies into the plains. A Pacific cold frontal passage helped transfer these winds to the surface and cold air advection on the sub-sident side of the trough maintained higher winds speeds for much of the day. Great Falls (76 mph), Helena (80 mph), and Havre (78 mph) all set wind wind speed records for the month of January. The winds were not only abnormally strong but they persisted longer than what is normally experienced, with some locations seeing frequent severe wind gusts for six hours or more. The impacts were felt throughout North-central and Southwest Montana and included, road closures due to areas of dense blowing dust, semi trailer roll overs, power outages, property damage, and even a few grass fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station EW1937 Goldstone (20 N Rudyard) measured a 70 kt (81 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river, or very moist plume of Pacific moisture, brought heavy mountain snowfall above 4,500 feet across the Idaho Panhandle into northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow developed at Lookout Pass by late evening, and continued through daybreak on the 3rd. The Montana Department of Transportation labeled the stretch of Interstate 90 from Lookout to Taft as severe driving conditions from 7 am to around 10 am MST. The Lookout SNOTEL reported 1.50 inches of snow water equivalent during this event, which equates to 12 to 14 inches of snowfall at the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer groomers for the Chief Joseph cross country trails had to clear an estimated 100 downed trees. They remarked that there was a tree down almost every 100 yards. The U.S. Forest Service reported that a couple of their radio repeaters were blown down in the Bitterroot Mountains. The Little Rock Creek RAWS located at 5500 feet, just south of Lake Como, reported a peak wind of 58 mph. A wind sensor on top of Mount Sentinel (north end of the Sapphire Mountains) recorded a peak wind speed of 125 mph at 10:45 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a very widespread wind event for northwest Montana. Countless trees were either blown down or snapped in half in many locations, causing numerous road blockages. At least 2 dozen trees were blown over along Bull Lake Road, 10 miles north of Heron. One power company reported that they had 51 broken power poles, and experienced extensive power line and cross arm damage during the event. Winds were gusting up to 80 mph at the Zonolite station which is situated 2,000 feet above the Libby area. Wind gusts between 66 and 70 mph were reported in the valleys in the Idaho Panhandle and based on the fact that trees were down all over Lincoln County, these wind speeds are a good estimate of what happened. At least 15,000 people were without power by mid-day on the 13th, and some outages lasted through the following weekend. One utility that covers part of the Idaho Panhandle and northwest Montana reported over one million dollars in damages.
