EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For central and western portions of Garfield County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph and the first severe wind gust of 61 mph were first recorded at the Lufborough Hill DOT site (MTLUF) at 4:07 PM MST.  The peak wind gust of 74 mph was also recorded there at 4:20 PM MST.  The final severe wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at Jordan airport ASOS site (KJDN) at 4:32 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and a strong jet stream over the region combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to bring some of those strong winds aloft, for multiple locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph and severe wind gusts of 58 mph began at the Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT site (MTLND) at 5:38 PM MST, then arrived at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR) around 6:30 PM MST.  The peak wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site from 6:38 PM to 6:44 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 50 and 65 mph in the lower elevations, causing mature trees to fall over or snap in half.  In Martin City, a mature tree snapped three feet from the ground and destroyed a small outbuilding. Power lines were reported down across Highway 83 about 4 miles northwest of Swan Lake. On top of Mount Aeneas, located in the Swan Range at nearly 7500 feet, winds peaked at 101 mph. The winds forced one ski resort to close the top half of their mountain. Several thousand people were without power, and the West Glacier School closed because of the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmospheric river brought heavy precipitation to the area.  This was followed by an anomalously strong mid-level jet stream that lowered to the surface behind a cold front and resulted in widespread wind damage across the Northern Rockies. Utilities reported over a million dollars in damages from this storm alone. The return interval for the mid-level winds of at least 80 mph occurring, was once every 30 years for early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple locations in both the Flathead and Mission valleys reported frequent gusts between 50 and 65 mph. An employee with the Montana Department of Transportation, who had lived in the area for over 40 years, remarked that he had never seen such widespread tree damage before. Many primary and secondary roads were blocked or closed for a time during the windstorm due to downed trees or powerlines down. One power company reported over 16,000 customers without power with 153 distinct outages from Libby to Essex at 3 pm MST on the 13th. Power was not restored for some people until 5 days later. A large pine tree crushed a pickup truck and damaged another vehicle on Elk Park Road in Columbia Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the week-long period of February 8-15, an arctic air mass settled over North-Central Montana, bringing one of the longest stretches of subzero temperatures in recorded history to the area (8 days at Great Falls, tied for 4th longest stretch). While winds were generally on the light side, they combined with the bitterly cold temperatures, resulting in wind chills below -40��F at times. These wind chills prompted Wind Chill Advisories and Wind Chill Warnings across the entire Great Falls County Warning Area. The core of the cold arrived for Wednesday through Friday, with air temperatures dropping to -30 or colder in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MCSCN Dahlman reports wind chill of -44.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds began to increase over North-Central and Southwestern Montana early on Sunday February 21 as a strong upper level jet roared overhead. Winds were generally strongest and most persistent across the plains during the day on Sunday, thanks to a strong pressure gradient that developed along the Rocky Mountain Front. After seeing widespread wind gusts over 60 mph, with isolated gusts in excess of 80 mph, during the day on Sunday, winds would generally begin to decrease late Sunday and early Monday closer to the Rocky Mountain Front, though winds around the Lewistown and Fort Belknap areas peaked early Monday morning, when some wind damage occurred in these areas. Winds area-wide would be a bit lighter during the day on Monday, but a final push of higher winds occurred late Monday night as a cold front pushed through the area. ||In addition to causing some damage across the plains, the strong winds also combined with a fairly fresh snowpack, which resulted in areas of intense blowing snow, which caused blizzard conditions along the Rocky Mountain Front. Areas of blowing snow also occurred in the Lewistown area, which would result in the closure of highway 191 between Harlowton and Judith Gap, but not before numerous traffic incidents had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sourdough mesonet reports 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a few quieter weather days across Central Montana, another upper level trough and attendant jet streak moved across the area. As this upper level trough moved through the area, a strong pressure gradient developed along the Rocky Mountain Front, which helped to promote the development of strong wind gusts across North-Central Montana, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front. Some light snow showers also occurred, but did not cause any widespread issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station dw4821, chester reports wind gust of 56 MPH.

Later that evening and night, larger-scale post-frontal non-thunderstorm high winds brought additional damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, multiple cases of thunderstorm wind damage: A stock trailer was rolled into a creek bottom; some shingles were blown off a roof; a barn wall was damaged; and a quonset door was lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the week-long period of February 8-15, an arctic air mass settled over North-Central Montana, bringing one of the longest stretches of subzero temperatures in recorded history to the area (8 days at Great Falls, tied for 4th longest stretch). While winds were generally on the light side, they combined with the bitterly cold temperatures, resulting in wind chills below -40��F at times. These wind chills prompted Wind Chill Advisories and Wind Chill Warnings across the entire Great Falls County Warning Area. The core of the cold arrived for Wednesday through Friday, with air temperatures dropping to -30 or colder in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ft. Belknap RAWS reports wind chill of -43.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds began to increase over North-Central and Southwestern Montana early on Sunday February 21 as a strong upper level jet roared overhead. Winds were generally strongest and most persistent across the plains during the day on Sunday, thanks to a strong pressure gradient that developed along the Rocky Mountain Front. After seeing widespread wind gusts over 60 mph, with isolated gusts in excess of 80 mph, during the day on Sunday, winds would generally begin to decrease late Sunday and early Monday closer to the Rocky Mountain Front, though winds around the Lewistown and Fort Belknap areas peaked early Monday morning, when some wind damage occurred in these areas. Winds area-wide would be a bit lighter during the day on Monday, but a final push of higher winds occurred late Monday night as a cold front pushed through the area. ||In addition to causing some damage across the plains, the strong winds also combined with a fairly fresh snowpack, which resulted in areas of intense blowing snow, which caused blizzard conditions along the Rocky Mountain Front. Areas of blowing snow also occurred in the Lewistown area, which would result in the closure of highway 191 between Harlowton and Judith Gap, but not before numerous traffic incidents had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT sensor just east of East Glacier Park reports 87 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary low pressure surface trough, warm moist air from the southeast and a strong cold front from the west all combined to allowed many severe thunderstorms to develop and strengthen through the afternoon and evening hours. Later that evening and night, larger-scale post-frontal non-thunderstorm high winds brought additional damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, a single-wide trailer home was flipped over and destroyed. Time and location are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, hail up to the size of baseballs.  Time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a persistent ridge of high pressure impacting most of the region for most of the month, most storm systems were directed away from northeast Montana, but a few helpful storms brought some slight relief to limited areas.  Overall, the current drought continued and mainly worsened as its severity spread more toward the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D3-Extreme) continued across all of Sheridan County through the entire month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, deep upper level trough set up over most of the CONUS, resulting in northwesterly flow across Montana. Multiple waves moved through the area embedded within this flow, resulting in periods of snow, which were heavy at times. In addition to the snow, winds would occasionally be gusty, and combined with low temperatures to occasionally produce low wind chills. However, wind chills warranting Wind Chill Warnings would not be seen until later in the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station mt-bv-18 reports 5.5IN of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front came over of the Continental Divide and collided with an atmospheric river of moisture from the Pacific Ocean to produce widespread wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, gusty winds, and snow bands brought difficult driving conditions. Snow amounts ranged from 2-4 inches in the Missoula Valley with 6-10 inches in the Bitterroot Valley. The southern Bitterroot Valley experienced the heaviest snow with 13-14 inches reported around Conner and Darby. Gusty winds and cold temperatures resulted in wind chills as low as -15F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front came over of the Continental Divide and collided with an atmospheric river of moisture from the Pacific Ocean to produce widespread wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow bands created severe driving conditions as reported by the state highway department near Lolo Pass and Lost Trail Pass. Snow totals ranged from 9 to 21 inches with wind gusts to 25 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front came over of the Continental Divide and collided with an atmospheric river of moisture from the Pacific Ocean to produce widespread wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, wind, blowing snow, and low wind chill values were all observed. A flash freeze occurred late on the 5th into the morning hours of the 6th causing icy conditions on Interstate 90. Periods of severe driving conditions were also reported by the state highway department through the morning of the 7th. Total snowfall ranged from 10-20 inches with the highest amount of 24 inches near Avon. Wind chills of -20F to -35F were noted as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front came over of the Continental Divide and collided with an atmospheric river of moisture from the Pacific Ocean to produce widespread wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind and heavy snow resulted in difficult driving conditions and natural avalanches in the backcountry. Snow totals ranged from 10-20 inches with the greatest total of 23.5 inches just east of Seeley Lake. Wind chill values of -10F to -20F were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second push of Arctic air came over the Continental Divide bringing windy conditions and low wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 20-30 miles per hour and well below zero air temperatures resulted in extended periods of wind chills of -30F to -50F. The coldest observed wind chill was -70F at the Snowslip sensor above US Highway 2.

Areas of blowing snow also occurred in the Lewistown area, which would result in the closure of highway 191 between Harlowton and Judith Gap, but not before numerous traffic incidents had occurred.

Areas of blowing snow also occurred in the Lewistown area, which would result in the closure of highway 191 between Harlowton and Judith Gap, but not before numerous traffic incidents had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rosenberg farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, deep upper level trough set up over most of the CONUS, resulting in northwesterly flow across Montana. Multiple waves moved through the area embedded within this flow, resulting in periods of snow, which were heavy at times. In addition to the snow, winds would occasionally be gusty, and combined with low temperatures to occasionally produce low wind chills. However, wind chills warranting Wind Chill Warnings would not be seen until later in the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 6 inches of snow so far. Snow continues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the week-long period of February 8-15, an arctic air mass settled over North-Central Montana, bringing one of the longest stretches of subzero temperatures in recorded history to the area (8 days at Great Falls, tied for 4th longest stretch). While winds were generally on the light side, they combined with the bitterly cold temperatures, resulting in wind chills below -40��F at times. These wind chills prompted Wind Chill Advisories and Wind Chill Warnings across the entire Great Falls County Warning Area. The core of the cold arrived for Wednesday through Friday, with air temperatures dropping to -30 or colder in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtnor norris hill (mt dot) reports wind chill of -40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds began to increase over North-Central and Southwestern Montana early on Sunday February 21 as a strong upper level jet roared overhead. Winds were generally strongest and most persistent across the plains during the day on Sunday, thanks to a strong pressure gradient that developed along the Rocky Mountain Front. After seeing widespread wind gusts over 60 mph, with isolated gusts in excess of 80 mph, during the day on Sunday, winds would generally begin to decrease late Sunday and early Monday closer to the Rocky Mountain Front, though winds around the Lewistown and Fort Belknap areas peaked early Monday morning, when some wind damage occurred in these areas. Winds area-wide would be a bit lighter during the day on Monday, but a final push of higher winds occurred late Monday night as a cold front pushed through the area. ||In addition to causing some damage across the plains, the strong winds also combined with a fairly fresh snowpack, which resulted in areas of intense blowing snow, which caused blizzard conditions along the Rocky Mountain Front. Areas of blowing snow also occurred in the Lewistown area, which would result in the closure of highway 191 between Harlowton and Judith Gap, but not before numerous traffic incidents had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delta AWOS reports 77 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front came over of the Continental Divide and collided with an atmospheric river of moisture from the Pacific Ocean to produce widespread wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow, wind, and dangerously low wind chills were observed. Snow amounts generally ranged from 5-14 inches with 22 inches reported near Marias Pass. Easterly wind gusts of 30-40 miles per hour resulted in blowing and drifting snow with wind chills of -15F to -30F at lower elevations and -35F to -55F at higher elevations. Dangerous avalanche conditions developed because of the wind and snow with one avalanche related fatality reported in the Swan Range near Wildcat Lake. This winter storm also created significant challenges for plow crews along US Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern through this mid-summer period continued to favor a strong ridge of hot high pressure over the western United States, and directed most storm systems well away from northeast Montana locations. Drought conditions showed very little or no improvement through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D2-Severe) were in the Little Rockies to start the month. During week two, a sliver of (D3-Extreme) moved into northern and west central Little Rockies with (D2-Severe) remaining elsewhere.  On the last week of July most of the Little Rockies worsened into (D3-Extreme) conditions, the exception being the far western corner which remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.

By mid month and through the end of June, D2-Severe drought replaced D3-Extreme drought through the west-central portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few helpful snow and rain showers brought a little relief to some areas through the month, but were not enough to weaken the drought's hold across the region.  Overall, precipitation amounts remained below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D2-Severe) in the western third of Northern Phillips County, and D3-Extreme drought in the eastern two thirds of Northern Phillips County continued through the entire month of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient, coupled with strong 60-80 knot winds at the mid levels, resulted in yet another round of gusty winds across the Plains of North-Central Montana, as well as a few areas in Southwest Montana, particularly in Madison County. Some snow also fell with this system, with 12 inches of snow reported at Teton Pass Ski Resort and 14 inches along the MT-ID border near West Yellowstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated wind gusts to 60 mph occurred in Southern Lewis and Clark County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a persistent ridge of high pressure impacting most of the region for most of the month, most storm systems were directed away from northeast Montana, but a few helpful storms brought some slight relief to limited areas.  Overall, the current drought continued and mainly worsened as its severity spread more toward the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start the month of June, ongoing drought conditions (D2-Severe) were across all but the southeastern edge of Garfield County while D3-Extreme remained in the far northeastern corner of the county (Haxby Point).  By the second week, D3-Extreme retreated eastward to leave all but the southeastern edge of Garfield County in D2-Severe.  By the third week of the month, D3-Extreme returned to central and northern portions of the county. By the end of the month, D3-Extreme spread further west to include most of Garfield county, except the southeast now in D2-Severe.

By the end of the month D3-Extreme has spread to more of the eastern and southern sections with D2-Severe holding on over western and northwestern sections.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thus far through the entire spring and early summer season, this area only experienced up to D1-Moderate drought, but by the third week of June, D2-Severe drought had spread through the Little Rocky Mountains, and remained there through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D2-Severe) across all of Petroleum County prevailed through the first three weeks of the month of June. By the end of the month, D3-Extreme drought had spread over all of Petroleum County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep Pacific trough initially brought H700 (mountain top) westerly winds of 50 to 80 kts on Sunday March 28th, 2021, which eclipsed the 99th percentile of climatology over many areas during the height of the storm. A tight surface pressure gradient, mountain wave activity, along with a well mixed atmospheric profile all contributed to transferring the elevated winds to ground level. Minimum relative humidity values fell into the 15 to 25 percent range as high temperatures warmed into the 60s to near 70 degrees. These conditions increased the risk for grassland fires and contributed to the explosive growth of the Greenhouse wildfire. On the backside of the storm, a cold front provided cold air advection and pressure rises for another round of strong winds Sunday night into Monday. Robust H700 cold air advection 60+ kt winds were also instrumental for the strong surface winds and snow shower development for that time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Belknap 13 S station measured a 59 kt (68 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1100 am MDT, a fast moving grass fire broke out northeast of the Browning area as winds gusted up to 70 mph over the area. Three structures were destroyed and the cost to contain the fire was about $100,000. The fire burned about 14,000 acres, before snow slowed it's growth during the evening and overnight hours. Crews continued fire containment through April 16th, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and pronounced cold front from the Canadian Rockies swept through the region with a strong jet stream loft. Scattered rain and snow showers aided in mixing these winds all the way down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At multiple locations across Richland County, sustained winds of at least 40 mph were common from the late morning through the late night of the 29th.  The peak wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at the Sioux Pass MT-16 DOT site (MTSOO).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greater than 400 customers lost power during this wind event. In general, the highest winds were in the mountains with a peak recorded gust of 52 mph at Zonolite. In populated locations, gusts into the 40s were common with a peak gust of 49 mph at Kila, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 200-500 customers lost power during the event. There was at least 1 report of a tree down north of Lakeside, MT. Winds were relatively uniform with widespread gusts into the 40s mph and a couple of reports in the low 50s mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With wind gusts into the 40s mph, impacts were constrained to 200 or fewer customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient, coupled with strong 60-80 knot winds at the mid levels, resulted in yet another round of gusty winds across the Plains of North-Central Montana, as well as a few areas in Southwest Montana, particularly in Madison County. Some snow also fell with this system, with 12 inches of snow reported at Teton Pass Ski Resort and 14 inches along the MT-ID border near West Yellowstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist surface conditions with temperature/dewpoints in the 80s/50s F respectively contributed to between 500 and 1000 J/kg of MLCAPE across much of south-central and southeast Montana. In addition, increased forcing for ascent from an upper level disturbance approaching from Idaho, along with effective shear of 40-50kts, resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms. This, combined with steep, low and|mid-level lapse rates, helped produce damaging wind gusts and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6 inch tree limb fell onto the road.

By the second week of September the far southeastern corner had worsened to (D3-Extreme) conditions to match the rest of eastern Richland County while the western three quarters of the county remained in (D4-Exceptional) drought.  These conditions remained the same the rest of the month.

A human-caused wildfire started on the afternoon of June 13th. The wildfire was located about 7 miles south of Red Lodge. Hot, dry, and windy conditions resulted in the fire to spread rapidly over the next 24 to 48 hours. The fire burned through the remainder of the month, as well as through the month of July. Acreage burned by the end of July was approximately 29,800 acres.   Over 30 structures were lost along with 2 primary residences. By the end of the month, the fire was 90% contained. For more information, please see InciWeb at https://inciweb.nwcg.gov.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative.

Some areas also saw some snowfall as the cold air pushed into the area. Some gusty winds occurred as the cold air made a temporary retreat, with isolated high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Norris Hill recorded a wind gust of 60 mph. This appears to have been an isolated report, with nearby stations recording wind gusts to around 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brought three days of thunderstorm activity that was followed by gusty westerly winds and low minimum relative humidity. This allowed the Harris Fire, located over southwestern Cascade County, to undergo explosive growth in just one day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harris Mountain Fire was a lightning caused fire discovered on July, 23rd. Gusty westerly winds and rugged terrain propelled the fire's burn area to 3,000 acres in less than one day. It prompted the evacuation of about 60 residents just a couple days after its discovery and was being managed by a type two incident team at that point. The fire ultimately burned 31,603. Estimated incident cost was $9,770,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Woods Creek Fire was lightning caused fire located in the Big Belt Mountains, discovered on July 10th. It was classified as a type one incident on July 31st, and resulted in evacuations on the eastern side of the fire. It ultimately burned 55,449 acres with an estimated incident cost of $27,000,000, as of January 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment had all the ingredients for strong to severe storms including above normal moisture and forecast shear in excess of 40 miles per hour, conducive to wind and hail threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-lived severe storm continued to produce 1 hail east of Seeley Lake. Additionally, it was reported the storm was producing heavy rain and winds up to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment had all the ingredients for strong to severe storms including above normal moisture and forecast shear in excess of 40 miles per hour, conducive to wind and hail threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This particular thunderstorm developed off the outflow boundary from a previous severe storm affecting Missoula and Powell Counties. The FBAN on the Anderson Hill Fire reported large hail and heavy rain had fallen on the fire. In fact, he said sufficient hail had fallen to cover the ground white. The storm continued to Monture Ranger Station where a ranger reported 2 hail and damage to vehicle windscreens (unknown number of vehicles).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west-southwest winds of 40-50kts occurred around 500mb. Lower/mid-tropospheric lapse rates were steep in response to insolation and weak mid-level cooling. In addition, there was sufficient low-level moisture to support weak to modest|CAPE on the order of 500-1000 J/kg. This resulted in the development of thunderstorms which produced mainly severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs 6 inches in diameter were broken.

An upper level trough with near record atmospheric moisture levels moved around the western U.S. ridge from Oregon to northwest Montana. This system initiated several waves of heavy rainfall over Glacier National Park which resulted in high elevation debris flows and impacted backpackers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Backpackers camping at the edge of Lake Ellen Wilson in Glacier National Park were awakened by torrential rainfall and the clapping of thunder around midnight. Bias-corrected radar data estimated over half an inch of rain fell in one hour through 1 am on the 11th. The rain continued at moderate intensities for a few more hours and ended by late morning with an radar estimated total rainfall amount of over 2 inches. The campers reported boulders rolling down Mount Jackson into the lake throughout the night with 6 new waterfalls cutting off their exit trail towards Sperry Chalet. Three backpackers had to bed down at Gunsight Pass for an extra night due to all the rock slides and fast-moving water through the trails.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into Montana the day after Christmas, bringing subzero temperatures and dangerous wind chills to the plains. Some areas also saw some snowfall as the cold air pushed into the area. Some gusty winds occurred as the cold air made a temporary retreat, with isolated high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesonet stations recorded wind chills between -40 and -50, with the coldest reading of -50 occurring 11 SSW of Whitlash at 12:21 pm on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Pacific Northwest began to compromise the ridge in place and introduced a more moist and unstable southwesterly flow to the Northern Rockies. This allowed daily rounds of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms to develop over Southwest and North-central MT. The period began with discrete supercell thunderstorms developing over the plains, producing severe wind gusts, large hail, and even a weak tornado. The strongest and most widespread activity occurred on July 7th as the Pacific trough and associated wind shear began to exert more of an influence on the region's weather and interacted with the moist and unstable environment already in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly to southeasterly near surface flow, to the cool side of a zone of stronger differential surface heating and deeper boundary-layer mixing to the east of the higher terrain, resulted in sufficient boundary-layer moisture to contribute to CAPE|on the order of 1000-1500 J/kg. In addition, a belt of 40+kt northwesterly flow around|500mb resulted in strong deep-layer shear. As a result, numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail developed across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

The lightning-caused fire started on July 17th in the northeast Crazy Mountains, 24 miles southwest of Harlowton. Driven by an unusual east wind, the fire made a significant run to the west late on July 30th but was stopped short of homes and other structures in the Shields River area. Total personnel on the fire was around 100 with 21,876 acres burned. Estimated containment is mid-October. Fuels involved were short grass and timber. For more information, please see the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Harris Wildfire north of Joliet, which was caused by lightning, burned around 2600 acres.|Road closures consisted of Coal Creek Road from Highway 212 to Bellion Road, all of Bellion Road and both entrances of Farewell Road. Multiple agencies responded to the fire. No structures were lost. There were approximately 116 personnel that worked on the fire.|A Red Lodge Fire engine was involved in a burn over event while doing initial attack on the fire. Scattered thunderstorms produced a wind shift around 430pm that resulted in the burn over. A Red Lodge firefighter was severely burned and flown to a Salt Lake City hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern through this mid-summer period continued to favor a strong ridge of hot high pressure over the western United States, and directed most storm systems well away from northeast Montana locations. Drought conditions showed very little or no improvement through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start the month of July, ongoing drought conditions (D3-Extreme) were across most of Central and Southern Valley County with (D2-Severe) in the far eastern portion. By the third week, the western third of Central and Southern Valley County had worsened to (D4-Exceptional Drought) with the middle third remaining in (D3-Extreme) conditions and the eastern third remaining in (D2-Severe) conditions through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions to start July were in (D3-Extreme) across all but the southeastern corner of Garfield County which was in (D2-Severe).  On the third week of July conditions across the northwestern corner worsened to (D4-Exceptional) with (D3-Extreme) remaining across most of central and southwestern Garfield County and (D2-Severe) remaining in the southeastern corner through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern through this mid-summer period continued to favor a strong ridge of hot high pressure over the western United States, and directed most storm systems well away from northeast Montana locations. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D3-Extreme) were in the eastern and southern sections of Central and Southeast Phillips County with D2-Severe over western and northwestern sections.  During the second week of July (D3-Extreme) conditions pushed west with only the far southwestern tip of Central and Southeast Phillips County remaining in (D2-Severe) conditions.  During the third week of July eastern sections of Central and Southeast Phillips County worsened into (D4-Exceptional) conditions.  On the last week the far southwestern tip of Central and Southeast Phillips County worsened into (D3-Extreme) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow boundary from thunderstorms moving north out of the Bitterroot Valley uprooted trees, created power outages, and damaged roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local apple orchard owner reported that 'a massive amount of flood water', overflowed the Bitterroot irrigation canal after heavy rain. This flood water carved 18 ditches through about 3 rows of his orchard. In addition, hail shredded some leaves of trees and strong winds toppled a 20-year old pine tree. After assessing the damage he felt there would be no crop of apples in 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wildfire was first reported on Tuesday, July 27 at 1:30 PM. It was then called Poverty Flats, named after the area ten miles north of Hardin where it originated from a burning coal seam. Due to a north wind from a cold front, the fire ran 16 miles south July 28 and 29. About 1:30 am July 29, the fire jumped the Sarpy Road (Highway 384) and entered the Crow Indian Reservation. The fire jumped the Little Big Horn River and threatened local homes. The fire burned 66,134 acres in grass and sage brush, and the final active areas were primarily in cottonwood trees along the river. The wildfire was fully contained on August 12th. For more detailed information, please see the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow boundary from thunderstorms moving north out of the Bitterroot Valley uprooted trees, created power outages, and damaged roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outflow wind from a northerly moving thunderstorm produced strong to severe winds from roughly 6-7 pm MDT, with a peak gust of 64 mph at Missoula Intl. Airport. These winds caused downed trees and powerlines, and also damaged a roof. The outflow boundary snapped a tree north of Seeley Lake at 7 MDT. The first reported downed tree happened at 5:52 pm MDT in the Mullan Trail area. Additionally, a homeless camp in South Missoula suffered ripped tents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Southwestern Montana in a warm, dry air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. These showers and thunderstorms produced widespread gusty outflow winds, including one that caused a 63 mph wind gust in Beaverhead County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT sensor 6 miles southeast of Jackson, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient, coupled with strong 60-80 knot winds at the mid levels, resulted in yet another round of gusty winds across the Plains of North-Central Montana, as well as a few areas in Southwest Montana, particularly in Madison County. Some snow also fell with this system, with 12 inches of snow reported at Teton Pass Ski Resort and 14 inches along the MT-ID border near West Yellowstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Norris hill recorded high wind gusts to 71 mph around for about an hour either side of noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern through this mid-summer period continued to favor a strong ridge of hot high pressure over the western United States, and directed most storm systems well away from northeast Montana locations. Drought conditions showed very little or no improvement through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions for McCone County were (D2-Severe) for the majority of the county, the exception being the far northwestern and far southeastern corners where (D3-Extreme) was occurring.  These conditions remained unchanged throughout the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern through this mid-summer period continued to favor a strong ridge of hot high pressure over the western United States, and directed most storm systems well away from northeast Montana locations. Drought conditions showed very little or no improvement through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions (D3-Extreme) occurred across most of Northern Valley County at the beginning of July, the exception being (D2-Severe) in the far southeast corner of Northern Valley County. These conditions remained unchanged throughout the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christensen Fire was discovered on July 16th in the Beaverhead/Deer Lodge National Forest. The fire grew to 5,442 acres and was zero percent contained on July 31st. The fire was located near the high profile Trailside and Alder Creek Fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient, coupled with strong 60-80 knot winds at the mid levels, resulted in yet another round of gusty winds across the Plains of North-Central Montana, as well as a few areas in Southwest Montana, particularly in Madison County. Some snow also fell with this system, with 12 inches of snow reported at Teton Pass Ski Resort and 14 inches along the MT-ID border near West Yellowstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds. Peak wind gust of 88 mph recorded by a mesonet station along the Rocky Mountain Front (dellwo).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday May 19, 2021, an upper level Pacific trough dove south through the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin and then became nearly stationary for a couple of days. The southerly flow ahead of this system transported middle and upper level moisture into the state, while an upslope east to northeast flow developed at the lower levels in response to relatively strong surface high and low pressure systems respectively set up over Alberta Canada and Utah.  The upslope flow eventually led to cold air damming and the development of a low level barrier jet along the Divide.  This resulted in strong northerly surface winds along eastern slopes adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front for periods of near blizzard conditions and subsequently sent the unseasonably cold air into the southwestern portion of the state.  Locations from Roger���s Pass northward into the East Glacier and Browning areas observed the worst conditions due to the combination of falling snow and gusty northerly winds. The impacts ranged from severe driving conditions on area roadways to isolated power outages. By Thursday morning, the rain began to change to snow at lower elevations over North-central Montana and this transition eventually reached the southwest valleys later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Military ASOS Foxtrot station K1FM observed visibility ranging from 1/4 SM to 1 SM in snow and wind for much of the am hours and early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific trough and attendant cold front brought a round of convective precipitation to much of Central and Southwest MT on Friday May, 7, 2021. Mid- level lapse rates exceeded 7 degrees C/km during the afternoon as effective shear briefly approached 50 kts and precipitable water values peaked at around 7 tenths of an inch. These ingredients were enough for a brief period of thunderstorms with severe wind gusts in the Bozeman area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down in city of Bozeman. Tress also fell on and damaged vehicles. Some fences also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving along the Canadian border interacted with moisture and instability over North-central MT for strong to severe thunderstorm development. Surface based CAPE was generally less than 500 j/kg, but effective shear above 30 kts and DCAPE as high as 2000 j/kg was enough to produce scattered areas of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at 18 E Fort Benton measured a 51 kt (59 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Pacific Northwest began to compromise the ridge in place and introduced a more moist and unstable southwesterly flow to the Northern Rockies. This allowed daily rounds of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms to develop over Southwest and North-central MT. The period began with discrete supercell thunderstorms developing over the plains, producing severe wind gusts, large hail, and even a weak tornado. The strongest and most widespread activity occurred on July 7th as the Pacific trough and associated wind shear began to exert more of an influence on the region's weather and interacted with the moist and unstable environment already in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell moved southeast across Blaine County early Tuesday|evening. This storm produced large hail of nearly two inches in|diameter along its path. As the severe storm approached Hays,|rotation strengthened slightly to where a brief tornado occurred.|The tornado occurred over open ranchland to the west of Montana|Highway 66 and, to our knowledge, no damage occurred. The|supercell then started to weaken as it move southeast between Hays|and Lodge Pole with the tornado likely lifting before it reached|Highway 66.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Goose Fire was located near Hoodoo Pass about 32 miles south of Ennis. The fire was  ignited by lightning on July 1st and burned through the month of July, though it was 80 percent contained by August 1st. The initial 250 acre fire was inaccessible to containment crews and was classified as a type 2 incident during its peak on July 10th. By the end of the month, it grew to 7,522 acres and at one structure was destroyed. The cost to fight the fire was about $7.6 million.

While snowfall amounts were generally 1-3 inches across the lower elevations, some of the mountains received upwards of a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station gnfhy 10.7 w emigrant (avalanche).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Black Mountain Fire was discovered on July 17th and grew to 1,021 acres by the end of the month. It was eventually managed by a type one incident team, along with the Alder and Trail Creek Fires, though its impacts were less than those major incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was the dominant weather pattern in July, which was occasionally suppressed by passing shortwaves and modest moisture for periods of scattered thunderstorms. Often times, the thunderstorm activity was followed by gusty winds and low relative humidity. Both lightning and human caused fires flourished in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The American Fork Fire was a lightning caused incident located in the Crazy Mountains. Only the northwest flank of this fire was located in Meagher County, with a majority of it residing over Park and  Sweet Grass Counties. By July 31st, the fire charred 14,397 acres and was only 10 percent contained at that time. This fire merged with the Ohearn Creek Fire on July 18th, 2021. At one point it became a part of the Divide Complex type 1 team managed by the Southern Area Incident Management team.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow continued into the middle of November over the Northern Rockies. H700 winds routinely exceeded 70 kts as surface pressure gradients assisted with mixing these winds to the surface. The strongest winds were initially observed over the Rocky Mountain Front, before expanding into the remainder of the region, when a strong Pacific trough moved through the region and a 982 mb surface low developed over the Canadian Prairies.  There were many reports of structural damage, power outages, and disruptions to travel, especially along the rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: La Crosse station at 6 NE Bozeman measured a 54 kt (62 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Canadian Rockies swept down through our region.  Atmospheric conditions were favorable to allow these strong and gusty winds to reach the surface across all portions of northeast Montana.  Wind gusts from this storm reached as high as 80mph and Glasgow recorded its highest ever wind gust for November at 69mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across McCone County, winds speeds of at least 40 mph were common from as early as the early morning to as late as the late evening hours.  The peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at the Fort Peck Dam mesonet site at 1:52 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very strong winds occurred for several days across the Billings Forecast Area. The strongest winds with accompanied damage occurred during the late evening of the 15th through the early afternoon hours on the 16th. A very strong jet stream overhead, along with rapid pressure rises behind a cold front, resulted in very strong, damaging winds. In addition, a mountain wave formed overnight over the Beartooth Mountains. This caused the strong jet stream winds aloft to be brought to the surface in an accelerated motion on the eastern side of the mountains. Red Lodge, MT was hit the hardest with wind gusts to 81 mph. ||A wildfire occurred during the wind storm, most likely due to downed power lines. The Rosebud Fire began at roughly 11:30pm on November 15th in the West Rosebud area. Evacuations for several areas were issued around 2am on November 16th, forcing dozens of people from their homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 70 mph. Damage was reported from Wilsall to Clyde Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow developed ahead of an approaching Pacific trough and brought strong to severe thunderstorms over a three day period.  Surface based CAPE routinely exceeded 1000 j/kg as steep mid-level lapse rates above 8C/km were common. Dry sub-cloud layers helped produce strong thunderstorm wind gusts and effective shear well above 30 kts was enough for quarter size hail and larger. The peak in severe weather occurred on Wednesday July 21st as the main energy associated with the trough moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-Op observer reported 1 inch hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In this southwest portion of Phillips County, sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common from the early to the late morning hours.  The peak wind gusts of 60 mph were recorded both at 3:41 AM at the Manning Corral Dogtown RAWS site and at 4:32 AM at the Zortman BLM mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very strong winds occurred for several days across the Billings Forecast Area. The strongest winds with accompanied damage occurred during the late evening of the 15th through the early afternoon hours on the 16th. A very strong jet stream overhead, along with rapid pressure rises behind a cold front, resulted in very strong, damaging winds. In addition, a mountain wave formed overnight over the Beartooth Mountains. This caused the strong jet stream winds aloft to be brought to the surface in an accelerated motion on the eastern side of the mountains. Red Lodge, MT was hit the hardest with wind gusts to 81 mph. ||A wildfire occurred during the wind storm, most likely due to downed power lines. The Rosebud Fire began at roughly 11:30pm on November 15th in the West Rosebud area. Evacuations for several areas were issued around 2am on November 16th, forcing dozens of people from their homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 58 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November once again saw precipitation amounts drier than average.  As a result extreme drought conditions persisted or worsened throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start November, ongoing drought conditions in Eastern Roosevelt county were at (D3-Extreme) in the northern two thirds of Eastern Roosevelt County as well as the southeastern corner while the southwestern third of Eastern Roosevelt County was at (D4-Exceptional) conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong low pressure to the north and strong high pressure to the south, a tight pressure gradient set up across our region, and allowed strong winds aloft to reach down to the surface, mainly near and south of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 11:15 AM and 11:45 AM, winds gusted over 58 mph at the BLM Zortman E mesonet site.  The peak wind gust at that site was 63 mph and occurred at 11:45 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November once again saw precipitation amounts drier than average.  As a result extreme drought conditions persisted or worsened throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the start of November the southern corner of Richland County was in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the northwestern third of the county was in (D4-Exceptional) drought and the central third of the county was in (D3-Extreme) conditions.  These conditions remained the same through the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into Montana the day after Christmas, bringing subzero temperatures and dangerous wind chills to the plains. Some areas also saw some snowfall as the cold air pushed into the area. Some gusty winds occurred as the cold air made a temporary retreat, with isolated high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall of 6 inches since 730 am mst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow continued into the middle of November over the Northern Rockies. H700 winds routinely exceeded 70 kts as surface pressure gradients assisted with mixing these winds to the surface. The strongest winds were initially observed over the Rocky Mountain Front, before expanding into the remainder of the region, when a strong Pacific trough moved through the region and a 982 mb surface low developed over the Canadian Prairies.  There were many reports of structural damage, power outages, and disruptions to travel, especially along the rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: BLM Virginia City station measured a 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow developed ahead of an approaching Pacific trough and brought strong to severe thunderstorms over a three day period.  Surface based CAPE routinely exceeded 1000 j/kg as steep mid-level lapse rates above 8C/km were common. Dry sub-cloud layers helped produce strong thunderstorm wind gusts and effective shear well above 30 kts was enough for quarter size hail and larger. The peak in severe weather occurred on Wednesday July 21st as the main energy associated with the trough moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KBZN measured a 59 kt (68 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

There were many reports of structural damage, power outages, and disruptions to travel, especially along the rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wise River station measured a 58 kt (67 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Canadian Rockies swept down through our region.  Atmospheric conditions were favorable to allow these strong and gusty winds to reach the surface across all portions of northeast Montana.  Wind gusts from this storm reached as high as 80mph and Glasgow recorded its highest ever wind gust for November at 69mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site, sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common from the early morning on November 16th, to the early morning on November 17th.  The peak wind gust of 80 mph was recorded from 6:01 PM to 6:09 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very strong winds occurred for several days across the Billings Forecast Area. The strongest winds with accompanied damage occurred during the late evening of the 15th through the early afternoon hours on the 16th. A very strong jet stream overhead, along with rapid pressure rises behind a cold front, resulted in very strong, damaging winds. In addition, a mountain wave formed overnight over the Beartooth Mountains. This caused the strong jet stream winds aloft to be brought to the surface in an accelerated motion on the eastern side of the mountains. Red Lodge, MT was hit the hardest with wind gusts to 81 mph. ||A wildfire occurred during the wind storm, most likely due to downed power lines. The Rosebud Fire began at roughly 11:30pm on November 15th in the West Rosebud area. Evacuations for several areas were issued around 2am on November 16th, forcing dozens of people from their homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 79 mph was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low moved over the Northern Rockies and provided relatively steep mid-levels lapse rates (7C/km+) while a dry sub-cloud layer maintained DCAPE values above 1000 j/kg. An area of relatively weak thunderstorms developed just west of the Townsend area and sent an outflow boundary moving toward the city. Local topography likely played a role in boosting wind gusts to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ginger RAWS near Townsend measured a 67 kt (77 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

By the end of August, the far southwestern corner had worsened to (D4-Exceptional) drought while the rest of the county remained in (D3-Extreme) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area, interacting with an unstable air mass that was in place ahead of it. This sparked strong thunderstorms along the front, which produced widespread wind gusts of 50+ mph and a few wind gusts over 58 mph. In addition, there were some strong winds along the front that were not caused by thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by ambient weather network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously high precipitable water values worked their way into Southwestern Montana, along with weak upper level flow. This combination resulted in slow moving heavy thunderstorms, which produced some impressive rainfall totals in places. For example, a meson site reported over 1.6 of rain, with 1.36 of that falling in just 20 minutes. This lead to some urban flash flooding, with a report out of Bozeman, MT of water flowing swiftly from south to north in the downtown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several social media reports of flash flooding in Downtown Bozeman, including swiftly flowing water from south to north in the downtown area. Additional flooding reported on the south side of town near 19th and Graf.

Most of the reports that were received were around 3/4, but there were two separate reports of 1 hail in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS reports 1 inch hail at Malmstrom Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wildfire was first reported on Tuesday, July 27 at 1:30 PM. It was then called Poverty Flats, named after the area ten miles north of Hardin where it originated from a burning coal seam. Due to a north wind from a cold front, the fire ran 16 miles south July 28 and 29. About 1:30 am July 29, the fire jumped the Sarpy Road (Highway 384) and entered the Crow Indian Reservation. The fire jumped the Little Big Horn River and threatened local homes. The fire burned 66,134 acres in grass and sage brush, and the final active areas were primarily in cottonwood trees along the river. For more detailed information, please see the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance that moved over the Billings Forecast area resulted in the development of a few thunderstorms that produced some severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm outflow passed over Crow Agency around 630pm with winds to 55 mph. A couple hours later, a shower with up to 20dbz moved over Crow Agency around 830pm, producing winds to 64 mph across a small area. A roof blew off an arbor, throwing it across the street, striking a camper in the head and throat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Richard Spring Fire started on Sunday, August 8th around 230pm MDT 10 miles southwest of Colstrip. It was caused by a coal seam. The wildfire burned 171,130 acres.|When the fire started, winds were gusting to 20 mph, but a strong cold front moved through the fire Sunday night with gusts to 45 mph. The fire resulted in Highways 212 and 39 to be closed at times for several days. Widespread power outages occurred for several days as well. The wildfire forced several evacuations in Lame Deer and Ashland along with several other small communities. In addition, 12 secondary structures were lost. For more detailed information, please see the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning-caused fire started on July 17th in the northeast Crazy Mountains, 24 miles southwest of Harlowton. Driven by an unusual east wind, the fire made a significant run to the west late on July 30th but was stopped short of homes and other structures in the Shields River area. Total personnel on the fire was around 100 with 21,892 acres burned.  Fuels involved were short grass and timber but primarily mixed conifer with timber liter and understory fuels. For more information, please see the latest GACC (Geographic Area Coordination Center) Detailed Situation Report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal subtropical moisture set the stage for thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall across southwest Montana. North of this moisture plume, the Boulder 2700 fire burned down structures near Montana Highway 35 along the southeastern shore of Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported heavy rainfall led to street flooding in the Uptown District of Butte, Montana. Further, road department officials reported several clogged storm drains resulted in areas of minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal subtropical moisture set the stage for thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall across southwest Montana. North of this moisture plume, the Boulder 2700 fire burned down structures near Montana Highway 35 along the southeastern shore of Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Boulder 2700 fire spread quickly during the early morning hours of August 1st in the presence of strong downslope winds and dry conditions. The fire ran down the west slope of the Mission Mountains, crossing Montana Highway 35, reaching the east shore of Flathead Lake. Damage surveys confirmed the loss of 14 primary structures and 17 outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of any significant precipitation during the normally wet months of March, April and May, combined with above normal temperatures going into June resulted in the beginning of a significant drought across the Billings Forecast Area. Temperatures across many areas were around 100 degrees by the middle of June. Temperatures during July and August were mainly in the 90s and lower 100s. This combined with very little, if any rainfall, resulted in extreme drought conditions across the entire area throughout most of the summer, with the hardest hit areas across south central and southeast Montana. Wetter conditions resulted in some improvement during the middle and latter part of August.|The following are some of the impacts resulting from the drought: (For more details, please see https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu)||-The entire Billings Forecast Area was designated Extreme (D4) Drought Conditions.|-The period May-July was some of the driest periods on record with extreme heat. |-It was one of the top 5 warmest summers on record.|-High percentages of short/very short topsoil and subsoil moisture conditions|-Lowest 5-10th percentiles for many stream and river flows per USGS|-The city of Billings had water restrictions, no watering on Mondays|-Low flows and warm temperatures resulted in fishing restrictions and closures per FWP|-Low crop yields (some of the lowest since 1988, some dating back to the 1930s)|-Hay shortages with many ranchers having to buy hay from out of state which was very |  costly.|-Poor pasture land|-Significant grasshopper activity in places and associated losses|-Low water supplies for cattle|-Low weights in calves and cows|-Selling off calves and cows early|-Above normal wildfire activity, along with an early start to the wildfire season|-Fire restrictions prohibited campfires|-Poor air quality from local and western U.S. wildfires|-Firefighters facing physical and mental exhaustion due to the extensive nature of initial |  attacks|-Agricultural producers dealing with the stress of the drought, some close to losing their |  livelihoods due to their crop/livestock losses.|-Impacts to mental health.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

Temperatures across many areas were around 100 degrees by the middle of June. Temperatures during July and August were mainly in the 90s and lower 100s. This combined with very little, if any rainfall, resulted in extreme drought conditions across the entire area throughout most of the summer, with the hardest hit areas across south central and southeast Montana. Wetter conditions resulted in some improvement during the middle and latter part of August.|The following are some of the impacts resulting from the drought: (For more details, please see https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu)||-The entire Billings Forecast Area was designated Extreme (D4) Drought Conditions.|-The period May-July was some of the driest periods on record with extreme heat. |-It was one of the top 5 warmest summers on record.|-High percentages of short/very short topsoil and subsoil moisture conditions|-Lowest 5-10th percentiles for many stream and river flows per USGS|-The city of Billings had water restrictions, no watering on Mondays|-Low flows and warm temperatures resulted in fishing restrictions and closures per FWP|-Low crop yields (some of the lowest since 1988, some dating back to the 1930s)|-Hay shortages with many ranchers having to buy hay from out of state which was very |  costly.|-Poor pasture land|-Significant grasshopper activity in places and associated losses|-Low water supplies for cattle|-Low weights in calves and cows|-Selling off calves and cows early|-Above normal wildfire activity, along with an early start to the wildfire season|-Fire restrictions prohibited campfires|-Poor air quality from local and western U.S. wildfires|-Firefighters facing physical and mental exhaustion due to the extensive nature of initial |  attacks|-Agricultural producers dealing with the stress of the drought, some close to losing their |  livelihoods due to their crop/livestock losses.|-Impacts to mental health.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced excessive rainfall over Big Sky Lake during the afternoon of August 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.3 inches of rainfall during a 45 minute period in Big Sky Lake. Excessive runoff washed out a resident's driveway along Big Sky Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 30, a weak shower moved over Bozeman, MT, and collapsed in the very dry air mass near the surface. The resulting downburst winds were measured at 79 mph at the Belgrade Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust came from convective clouds with no lightning observed. KTFX/KBLX radars showed only 20 to 25 DBZ echoes.

Wetter conditions resulted in some improvement during the middle and latter part of August.|The following are some of the impacts resulting from the drought: (For more details, please see https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu)||-The entire Billings Forecast Area was designated Extreme (D4) Drought Conditions.|-The period May-July was some of the driest periods on record with extreme heat. |-It was one of the top 5 warmest summers on record.|-High percentages of short/very short topsoil and subsoil moisture conditions|-Lowest 5-10th percentiles for many stream and river flows per USGS|-The city of Billings had water restrictions, no watering on Mondays|-Low flows and warm temperatures resulted in fishing restrictions and closures per FWP|-Low crop yields (some of the lowest since 1988, some dating back to the 1930s)|-Hay shortages with many ranchers having to buy hay from out of state which was very |  costly.|-Poor pasture land|-Significant grasshopper activity in places and associated losses|-Low water supplies for cattle|-Low weights in calves and cows|-Selling off calves and cows early|-Above normal wildfire activity, along with an early start to the wildfire season|-Fire restrictions prohibited campfires|-Poor air quality from local and western U.S. wildfires|-Firefighters facing physical and mental exhaustion due to the extensive nature of initial |  attacks|-Agricultural producers dealing with the stress of the drought, some close to losing their |  livelihoods due to their crop/livestock losses.|-Impacts to mental health.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, dry air remained over Southwestern Montana on August 17 as a cold front continued to gradually push into the area. The approaching front helped to trigger another round of afternoon showers and thunderstorms across SW Montana, which would go on to produce another round of gusty outflow winds, including a wind gust to nearly 70 mph at the Dillon Airport. In addition, the frontal passage in Bozeman produced some strong wind gusts, with the Belgrade airport reporting a 63 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dillon ASOS reports wind gust of 69 mph.

The approaching front helped to trigger another round of afternoon showers and thunderstorms across SW Montana, which would go on to produce another round of gusty outflow winds, including a wind gust to nearly 70 mph at the Dillon Airport. In addition, the frontal passage in Bozeman produced some strong wind gusts, with the Belgrade airport reporting a 63 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Belgrade ASOS reports wind gust of 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Strong pressure rises behind the front resulted in very high wind gusts for several hours across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Miles City Airport gusted to 64 mph for 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dug into the Northwestern United States during the day on October 11th, and dug southeast. As this system moved south of the Great Falls CWA, moisture on the northern edge of this system brought the first widespread accumulating snowfall to most of Southwestern Montana, with some locations seeing over a foot of snowfall accumulation. As the snow fell, scattered power outages occurred across Southwestern Montana, particularly in the Bozeman area, and multiple motor vehicle accidents and subsequent delays occurred, particularly in and around Bozeman Pass. The majority of the snow fell over Madison, Beaverhead, and Gallatin Counties, though lighter amounts (up to 4 inches at lower elevations) did fall as far north and east as Fergus County.||Key Impacts: Heavy Snow, traffic accidents and delays, power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 16 inches measured 5 miles east of Gallatin Gateway, with drifts 3 to 4 feet deep in spots. In addition, Northwestern Energy reported numerous power outages in the Bozeman area at 2PM MDT/3PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dug into the Northwestern United States during the day on October 11th, and dug southeast. As the snow fell, scattered power outages occurred across Southwestern Montana, particularly in the Bozeman area, and multiple motor vehicle accidents and subsequent delays occurred, particularly in and around Bozeman Pass. The majority of the snow fell over Madison, Beaverhead, and Gallatin Counties, though lighter amounts (up to 4 inches at lower elevations) did fall as far north and east as Fergus County.||Key Impacts: Heavy Snow, traffic accidents and delays, power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop station madm8 measured 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving along the Canadian border interacted with moisture and instability over North-central MT for strong to severe thunderstorm development. Surface based CAPE was generally less than 500 j/kg, but effective shear above 30 kts and DCAPE as high as 2000 j/kg was enough to produce scattered areas of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines reported along highway 87 near Verona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief lull in the winds, strong, gusty winds returned to the Rocky Mountain Front once again on October 28. While winds were not as widespread as they were the day before, a few areas along the Rocky Mountain Front still saw wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station DELMT (Dellwo) measured a peak wind gust of 82 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing dry conditions, along with unseasonably warm and gusty winds, allowed the human-caused Crown Mountain and South Moccasin Fires to quickly grow on the 4th of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crown Mountain Fire started within the Forest boundary of the Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest some 15 miles southwest of Augusta, MT. During the initial three days, gusty winds, unseasonably high temperatures, and dry conditions resulted in active fire behavior and dangerous firefighting conditions for direct attack on the fire. These circumstances forced air and ground crews to disengage from the fire on 10/5 and 10/6. The fire is burning within portions of the footprint of the 1988 Canyon Creek Fire. The containment withstood winds exceeding 50mph on 10/9 and 10/10 ahead of a cold front that moved in. From 10/11 until present cooler conditions have prevailed with 2 inches of snow falling on the fire area on 10/12. |As of mid-November, the fire was 30% contained and had burned nearly 1,400 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing dry conditions, along with unseasonably warm and gusty winds, allowed the human-caused Crown Mountain and South Moccasin Fires to quickly grow on the 4th of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Moccasin Fire began on October 4, 2021 around 12:45, and quickly grew in size over the course of the afternoon in unseasonably warm, dry, and breezy conditions. This fire would quickly consume a large portion of the South Moccasin range, along with 5 structures (these were not primary residences). The NWR transmitter for Lewistown sustained some damage, but remained operational.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight north/south pressure gradient resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 59 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight north/south pressure gradient resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 mph across the Molt area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight north/south pressure gradient resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 59 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Wind gusts occurred along the Beartooth Foothills during the early evening on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 75 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September rainfall amounts were among the driest on record.  This allowed drought conditions in northeast Montana to persist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, ongoing drought conditions in the southwestern half of western Roosevelt County including the western border with Valley County were at (D4-Exceptional) while the northeastern half of western Roosevelt County remained in (D3-Extreme) conditions.  By the end of the month the northern two thirds of western Roosevelt County improved to (D3-Extreme) conditions while the southwestern third of western Roosevelt County remained at (D4-Exceptional) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through the area on October 12th - 13th.  The southeastern zones received a good amount of rain and snowfall to help drought conditions, however the rest of the area only got a little precipitation.  So despite this welcome storm, the extreme drought continues over northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start October, ongoing drought conditions in Sheridan County were at (D3-Extreme) for the whole county. These conditions remained the same through the rest of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through the area on October 12th - 13th.  The southeastern zones received a good amount of rain and snowfall to help drought conditions, however the rest of the area only got a little precipitation.  So despite this welcome storm, the extreme drought continues over northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start October, ongoing drought conditions were at (D4-Exceptional) conditions over the western three quarters of Richland County, with (D3-Extreme) conditions along the eastern border with North Dakota.  On the third week, the southeastern half of Richland County improved to (D3-Extreme) conditions, with the far southeastern corner of Richland County improving further to (D2-Severe) conditions, while the northwestern half of the county remained in (D4-Exceptional) conditions.  At the end of October the southern corner of Richland County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions, while the northwestern third of the county remained in (D4-Exceptional) drought and the central third of the county remained in (D3-Extreme) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through the area on October 12th - 13th.  The southeastern zones received a good amount of rain and snowfall to help drought conditions, however the rest of the area only got a little precipitation.  So despite this welcome storm, the extreme drought continues over northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start the month of October, ongoing drought conditions in the eastern two thirds of Prairie County were (D4-Exceptional) conditions, with the western third in (D3-Extreme) conditions.  By the third week of October, all of Prairie County had improved to (D3-Extreme) conditions.  By the end of the month the eastern quarter and along the northern border of Prairie County remained in (D3-Extreme) conditions, but the rest of the county had improved to (D2-Severe) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dug into the Northwestern United States during the day on October 11th, and dug southeast. As this system moved south of the Great Falls CWA, moisture on the northern edge of this system brought the first widespread accumulating snowfall to most of Southwestern Montana, with some locations seeing over a foot of snowfall accumulation. As the snow fell, scattered power outages occurred across Southwestern Montana, particularly in the Bozeman area, and multiple motor vehicle accidents and subsequent delays occurred, particularly in and around Bozeman Pass. The majority of the snow fell over Madison, Beaverhead, and Gallatin Counties, though lighter amounts (up to 4 inches at lower elevations) did fall as far north and east as Fergus County.||Key Impacts: Heavy Snow, traffic accidents and delays, power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer grnm8 5 miles SSE of Grant reports 6.5 inches of snow. Higher amounts were observed in the mountains, with some Snowtel sites reporting over 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first snow occurred during the second week of October. A surface low moved northeast out of the Four Corners region across Wyoming into the northern high plains. This allowed cold air to move south out of Canada while drawing moisture northward from the Gulf of Mexico. However, most of the cold air was due to dynamic cooling as a result of this powerful low. Heavy snow occurred across the entire Billings Forecast Area with the heaviest occurring across southeast Montana close to the Dakota and Wyoming borders. In addition, very strong northwest winds on the back side of the low resulted in significant blowing and drifting snow across southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported, along with blowing and drifting snow. Key Impacts: Poor driving conditions were reported. U.S. 12 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September rainfall amounts were among the driest on record.  This allowed drought conditions in northeast Montana to persist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, ongoing drought conditions were at (D4-Exceptional) for the northern three quarters of Petroleum County, while the southern quarter of Petroleum County remained in (D3-Extreme) conditions. These conditions remained the same through the end of September.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, ongoing drought conditions were at (D3-Extreme) conditions for all of the Little Rocky Mountains. These conditions remained the same through the end of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through the area on October 12th - 13th.  The southeastern zones received a good amount of rain and snowfall to help drought conditions, however the rest of the area only got a little precipitation.  So despite this welcome storm, the extreme drought continues over northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start October, ongoing drought conditions in all of Daniels County were at (D3-Extreme).  These conditions remained the same through the rest of October.
