EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow developed ahead of an approaching Pacific trough and brought strong to severe thunderstorms over a three day period.  Surface based CAPE routinely exceeded 1000 j/kg as steep mid-level lapse rates above 8C/km were common. Dry sub-cloud layers helped produce strong thunderstorm wind gusts and effective shear well above 30 kts was enough for quarter size hail and larger. The peak in severe weather occurred on Wednesday July 21st as the main energy associated with the trough moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KHVR measured a 50 kt (58 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds arrived across North-Central Montana during the overnight hours on Wednesday October 27 as an upper level system and associated strong winds arrived. Although winds would initially be confined to the Rocky Mountain Front during the overnight hours, high winds would spread across the plains of North-Central Montana during the daytime as thermal mixing transported the stronger winds aloft to the surface. In addition, the winds did result in a few areas of blowing dust, which caused a few isolated areas of reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet KCII (Choteau) measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large arctic air mass descended southward from Canada to spread across our region for multiple days.  Re-enforcing cold low pressure disturbances continued to rotate along the edge of this air mass, which supported this prolonged dangerously cold period. For most of these days, enough wind was present to create dangerously cold wind chill values for may locations across northeast Montana, and persisted through New Year's Eve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Richland County, temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse from early morning of the 27th into the late night of the 31st. The peak wind chill of -45 was recorded at 10:16 PM on the 31st at the Badger Creek CWOP Mesonet site (MTMCD).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 27, two snowmobilers were caught and killed in an avalanche on Scotch Bonnet Mountain, north of Cooke City. A group of 8 snowmobilers from Minnesota were on their second day of riding when two of their machines got stuck on the southeast face of Scotch Bonnet Mountain. Two members of their group rode up to help them dig out and the avalanche was triggered soon after. The avalanche broke 4-5 feet deep, ran 300 feet wide, and 500 feet vertical and killed two riders, burying them under 4-5 feet of debris. Everyone had rescue equipment and they were recovered by their party. For a more detailed report, please see www.mtavalanche.com.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative.

Most of the severe weather reports were hail, with the largest hail report in the Great Falls CWA coming in around 2.5 inches near the eastern border of Blaine County (this storm would go on to produce 3 inch hail in Phillips County in Glasgow's CWA). Severe thunderstorms lasted until approximately 11:30 PM, at which point the thunderstorms that remained in the area had weakened below severe thresholds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time is approximate. Part of tree was taken down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through Montana on June 15th, bringing some gusty winds as high pressure quickly moved into the area. This strong pressure gradient combined with the terrain to produce a few reports of wind gusts in excess of 60mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station delmt dellwo (mcscn).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough passed through the area, bringing a wetting rain to a large portion of the area. There was just enough instability as this system moved through that a cold air funnel formed east of Great Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold air funnel reported 4 miles east of observers location. Photo provided on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed across the Plains of North-Central Montana during the late afternoon and early evening, mainly east of a line from Great Falls to Havre. Most of the severe weather reports were hail, with the largest hail report in the Great Falls CWA coming in around 2.5 inches near the eastern border of Blaine County (this storm would go on to produce 3 inch hail in Phillips County in Glasgow's CWA). Severe thunderstorms lasted until approximately 11:30 PM, at which point the thunderstorms that remained in the area had weakened below severe thresholds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of quarter sized hail. Time estimated based on radar.

An upper level disturbance move through the area in the wake of a cold front, which allowed for a few thunderstorms to pop up during the afternoon. Isolated severe wind gusts and hail fell from some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts estimated around 60mph with sustained winds around 30 to 40 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance move through the area in the wake of a cold front, which allowed for a few thunderstorms to pop up during the afternoon. Isolated severe wind gusts and hail fell from some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports 1 to 1.25  inch hail south of Hobson near Black Bull RD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front ushered an arctic air mass into Montana on the evening of the 16th/morning of the 17th. As this air mass moved into the area, gusty winds combined with the cold air, bringing widespread wind chills to -30 to -40, with some areas seeing even colder wind chills. In addition to the cold, some snow also fell, especially across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 12 inches of new snow overnight at Showdown Ski resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient, coupled with strong 60-80 knot winds at the mid levels, resulted in yet another round of gusty winds across the Plains of North-Central Montana, as well as a few areas in Southwest Montana, particularly in Madison County. Some snow also fell with this system, with 12 inches of snow reported at Teton Pass Ski Resort and 14 inches along the MT-ID border near West Yellowstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Teton Ski resort in the mountains reported 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Deep Creek Fire began on June 13, 2021, likely due to a tree falling on a power line. While occasional critical fire weather conditions helped the blaze to grow and spread, the fire was 95% contained on June 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Woods Creek Fire began on June 13th when a fallen tree caused a downed power line. Over the course of the fire, three single-family homes were destroyed, along with four other structures. In addition, a Montana DNRC helicopter fighting the fire crash-landed, causing it to catch on fire. All five people on board survived the crash with minor injuries. Total cost of fighting the fire was estimated to be $5.8 million USD as of January 2022. An estimated 4,648 acres burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms crossed the Continental Divide during the afternoon of June 3 and strengthened as they moved over the plains of North-Central Montana. The storms appear to have peaked in the Great Falls area, where a few trees were damaged and some flash flooding occurred. These storms weekend as they moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Newspaper reports flash flooding occurred along 3rd St NW between the Fairgrounds and the Montana Department of Transportation Yard. Waters on the street reached 18 inches in depth and impeded access to several businesses in the area. Several motorists were stranded, and water began rising up through manholes in the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms crossed the Continental Divide during the afternoon of June 3 and strengthened as they moved over the plains of North-Central Montana. The storms appear to have peaked in the Great Falls area, where a few trees were damaged and some flash flooding occurred. These storms weekend as they moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video posted on Facebook shows a tree getting blown down on Skyline Drive NE. Some area Weather Underground weather station reported peak wind gusts of 50-55 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-Duty NWS employee reports some trees down in the stone meadows neighborhood on the north side of Great Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance move through the area in the wake of a cold front, which allowed for a few thunderstorms to pop up during the afternoon. Isolated severe wind gusts and hail fell from some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Awos station KMVH N Bar Ranch. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Montana on June 10, bringing a few isolated thunderstorms and gusty winds to the area. One of the thunderstorms would become severe, dropping 1 inch hail near Grass Range, MT. In addition, winds across SW Montana were quite strong near and behind a cold front, with many reports of wind gusts in excess of 50 MPH, and a few wind gusts over 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch hail from MPING.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS reports 61 mph peak gusts recorded at the dillon airport at 1:53 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds sustained 38 to 45 mph throughout the afternoon and evening. Regular gusts into mid 50s mph. Peak gust to 66 mph at 3pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of June 22, a thunderstorm began to decay near Dillon, MT. This thunderstorm produced an outflow with winds of 60 MPH at the Dillon Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a decaying thundershower. Dillon Airport ASOS KDLN.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtm07 3.2 sw amsterdam (mt-meso).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through Montana on June 15th, bringing some gusty winds as high pressure quickly moved into the area. This strong pressure gradient combined with the terrain to produce a few reports of wind gusts in excess of 60mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Asos station kctb cut bank airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranged between 45 and 56 mph, which caused a tree to fall onto a house and vehicle in Martin City, and multiple trees down along US-2 between West Glacier and Essex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds across the area led to a few thousand power outages. Additionally, there were a couple reports of trees blown down, part of a roof ripped off in the Upper Miller Creek area, and transmission line damage in the Missoula area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of roof damage and blown over trees near Skalkaho Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported multiple trees on power lines leading to many outages across the region. Some of those power outages lasted up to 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed in moist northwest flow behind a passing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple accidents and car slide offs occurred within 1 hour of snow squalls affecting both the I-90 and US-93 highways from Missoula south through Stevensville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed in moist northwest flow behind a passing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls led to multiple accidents and slide offs, mainly along I-90 from Drummond to Deer Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was observed across Lincoln and Flathead Counties during the morning hours of December 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice from freezing rain impacted travel in the Flathead Valley from Kalispell to Bigfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, followed by a period of freezing rain was observed along the Highway 200 corridor near the Idaho border. Valley locations near the Idaho border received five to eight inches of snow, with 15 inches at Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snowfall across Northwest Montana and the Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow led Montana Department of Transportation officials to declare severe driving conditions on US Highway 12 over Lolo Pass from milepost 0.0 to 4.0 during the morning hours of December 12th. The Lolo Pass Snotel measured 0.40 inches of precipitation between 800 am and noon, suggesting snowfall rates of one inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall rates around one inch per hour led the Montana Department of Transportation to declare severe driving conditions on US Highway 12 from mile markers 0.0 to 4.0 over Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow during the morning commute led to numerous accidents, especially across the Missoula Valley. Dangerous travel conditions led to a two hour delay for Ravalli County school districts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved over the Divide, leading to heavy snow and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy snow showers with snowfall rates around one inch per hour impacted Lookout Pass Christmas Eve. A crash closed I-90 westbound on the Idaho side of Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to twelve inches of wet heavy snow was observed across valley areas, with one to two feet of snow impacting mountain areas above 5,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow was measured across the Flathead Valley, with little to any accumulation across the Mission Valley. Snow changed to freezing rain by the early morning hours of December 19th. Trained spotters reported a light glaze of ice on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow and freezing rain was observed across the Missoula and Bitterroot valleys. One to three inches of snow along with a light glaze of ice made for slick travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snow led to travel impacts. Two to five inches of snow was observed across valley areas. Finally, a light glaze of ice was observed along the I-90 corridor near Clinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture interacted with an arctic air mass leading to widespread heavy snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snow led to travel delays along the I-90 corridor. Two to five inches of snow was observed across valley areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was observed across Lincoln and Flathead Counties during the morning hours of December 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain impacted US Highway 93 from Stryker to Whitefish. The public reported slick conditions and slow travel times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was observed across Lincoln and Flathead Counties during the morning hours of December 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was reported in Libby during the morning commute of December 8th. Residents reported slick roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was observed across Lincoln and Flathead Counties during the morning hours of December 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was reported in the Flathead Valley during the morning commute of December 7th. Impacts were observed at Glacier Park International Airport, as officials noted a ton of deicer was needed. A trained spotter reported a glaze of ice on windshields in Columbia Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed across the Bitterroot Valley as a Pacific cold front tracked across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 54 mph was observed Hamilton. Isolated power outages impacted the Bitterroot Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of Arctic air came over the Continental Divide bringing windy conditions and low wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 15-25 miles per hour and well below zero air temperatures resulted in extended periods of wind chills of -30F to -50F. The coldest observed wind chill was -52F at the Snowslip sensor above US Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds led to power outages across portions of Northwest Montana on December 1st.  A Pacific jet was present across the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. Unstable conditions allowed for gusty winds to mix down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly wind gusts of 40 mph or higher were observed at Marias Pass from midnight to 600 am on December 1st. A peak gust of 59 mph was observed at midnight. The Montana Department of Transportation reported a large tree blocking the eastbound lane of US Highway 2 at milepost 176. Flathead Electric COOP reported 353 residents without power in the vicinity of Essex, with power being restored by 619 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds led to power outages across portions of Northwest Montana on December 1st.  A Pacific jet was present across the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. Unstable conditions allowed for gusty winds to mix down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were observed in the Kootenai/Cabinet region during the morning and afternoon hours of December 1st. The Flathead Electric COOP reported two power outages associated with tree damage, totaling 298 residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snowfall across Northwest Montana and the Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation officials reported eight to ten inches of snow impacted US Highway 2 from Essex to Devils Creek on Saturday, with another eight to twelve inches falling by Sunday morning. Mesonet sites from Essex to Marias Pass measured an eight to twelve inch snow depth increase. Southwest wind gusts up to 33 mph were observed at Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snowfall across Northwest Montana and the Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain and wet snow was observed across valley locations, with generally three to six inches of snow being reported. Mountain area SNOTEL sites measured around one foot of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snowfall across Northwest Montana and the Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow led Montana Department of Transportation officials to declare severe driving conditions on I-90 over Lookout Pass from milepost 0.0 to 5.0 during the morning hours of December 12th. Numerous accidents occurred, leading to travel delays across Lookout Pass. The Mullan Pass ASOS measured 2.59 inches of precipitation during this event; suggesting at least 18 inches of snow fell for nearby Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved over the Divide, leading to heavy snow and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snow was observed across the West Glacier region Christmas Eve, with snowfall rates around two inches per hour. Temperatures quickly dropped into the single digits as an arctic front moved over the Divide early Christmas morning. Snow and blowing snow was observed on US 2, with wind chills of -10 to -20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front brought heavy snow, gusty winds, and a quick drop in temperature across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, wind gusts to 30 mph, and falling temperatures led the Montana Department of Transportation to close I-90 over Homestake Pass between 424 pm and 550 pm. Two inches of snow was measured along the I-90 corridor from Deer Lodge to Butte during a 30 minute period as the front passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds led to 400 to 500 power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused power outages along both I-90 and the MT-200 corridors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, upper level ridge, with strong warming and record temperatures in the 50s and 60s, ahead of a strong cold front set the stage for a damaging high wind event across the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down, some damaging power lines, due to high winds along US-2 and US-93. A Facebook dashcam video showed a vehicle hitting a falling tree during the storm. Several thousand citizens were without power for close to 12 hours after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe convective line moved through the Great Falls area as a cold front pushed through the area. As the line moved through, precipitation quickly switched to snow, and roads quickly iced over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Gary Cooper bridge near Dearborn reports wind gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet streak moved over North-Central Montana, bringing powerful winds to the area, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts along the Rocky Mountain Front exceeded 100 mph in some locations, particularly at Deep Creek and at the Two Medicine Bridge along US 2. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph spread across the plains, with most of North-Central Montana seeing High Wind Criteria. Of note, several fires occurred during this wind event. More information on these fires are under the episode titled Wind-fueled fires sweep across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest Energy meson 10 miles west of Simms reports a wind gust of 64 mph multiple times through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet streak moved over North-Central Montana, bringing powerful winds to the area, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts along the Rocky Mountain Front exceeded 100 mph in some locations, particularly at Deep Creek and at the Two Medicine Bridge along US 2. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph spread across the plains, with most of North-Central Montana seeing High Wind Criteria. Of note, several fires occurred during this wind event. More information on these fires are under the episode titled Wind-fueled fires sweep across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station gw0625 measures peak wing gust of 62 mph. Multiple other stations gusted over 58 mph through the period indicated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds continued to howl across the plains as December began, bringing widespread impacts to the region. These winds combined with an ongoing drought and resulting very dry fine fuels to create conditions ripe for the rapid spread and growth of fires that started. By 6am, crews were working at least three fires across North-Central Montana, one just south of Great Falls, one near Denton, and one near Browning. See episode titled High Winds to Start December for more information on the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire near Blackfoot, Montana was reported at 4:22 am on December 1st, fueled by dry grasses and wind gusts over 90 mph. Despite these strong winds and dry grasses, fire officials were able to control the fire by 11am, allowing residents to return to their homes. No homes were lost to the blaze, but a barn was damaged as the fire burned 780 acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Wind Fire was first reported on Tuesday, November 30, 2021 as a start in Judith Basin County that quickly spread into Fergus County. High winds with gusts over 60 mph pushed the fire into the town of Denton, MT where multiple structures were threatened. 25 primary structures, 18 secondary structures and 6 commercial structures are destroyed, along with 100,000 bushels of recently harvested grain that burned in two grain elevators. The fire was caused by a powerline.

By 6am, crews were working at least three fires across North-Central Montana, one just south of Great Falls, one near Denton, and one near Browning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on the south side of Great Falls early in the morning of December 1st as the result of arson. This fire quickly grew with winds gusting to 50-60 mph, and spread into the Gibson Flats neighborhood, where it caused up to 65 people to be evacuated from their homes, and would ultimately burn 11 homes, 7 garages, and 11 outbuildings as the fire burned 112 acres before it was able to be contained. To date, this is Great Fall's most destructive wild fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet streak moved over North-Central Montana, bringing powerful winds to the area, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts along the Rocky Mountain Front exceeded 100 mph in some locations, particularly at Deep Creek and at the Two Medicine Bridge along US 2. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph spread across the plains, with most of North-Central Montana seeing High Wind Criteria. Of note, several fires occurred during this wind event. More information on these fires are under the episode titled Wind-fueled fires sweep across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station at N Bar Ranch reports wind gust of 75 mph.
